Plot Studio definition changes

Side-by-side comparison of the repository baseline and the reviewed definitions now installed in jamovi. Red strike-through text was removed; green text was added. Unchanged definitions are omitted.

116 glossary entries changed77 linked definitions changed193 total comparisons
Glossary

p value

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Dist, Corr, Likert, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
The p value is the probability of getting a result at least as extreme as the one you saw, IF there were really no effect (the null hypothesis were true). A small p means your data would be surprising in a world with no effect.
A p value asks: if the stated null hypothesis and the test's assumptions were true, how often would the test statistic be at least as extreme as the one observed? A smaller p means the data are less compatible with that null model.
How to read it
Smaller means more surprising under the null. Read it against your cutoff (often .05), and remember it is reported with no leading zero, so p = .03 means 0.03.
Compare p with the alpha level chosen for the analysis; p = .03 means 0.03. When practical, report the exact p together with the effect estimate and its uncertainty.
Common misread / caution
It is NOT the probability that the null is true, that your result is due to chance, or that the effect is real; it only assumes the null and asks how surprising your data would be.
A p value is not the probability that the null is true, that chance caused the data, or that an effect is real. It also does not measure effect size or scientific importance.
Glossary

p adjusted

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Corr

Before
After
Definition
An adjusted p value is a p value recalculated to account for the fact that you ran several tests at once. Depending on the correction, it controls either the chance of any false positive across the set or the expected share of false positives among the results you flag. When you compare many pairs, use this column, not the raw p.
An adjusted p value accounts for a defined family of several tests. Bonferroni, Holm, and Benjamini-Hochberg modify the raw p values. Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett instead calculate multiplicity-adjusted p values from their own comparison models.
How to read it
Compare it to your cutoff (often .05) exactly as you would a raw p; the correction is already built in. It sets a stricter bar, so expect it to run higher than the raw p.
Compare the reported value directly with alpha, and state which adjustment was used and which tests formed the family.
Common misread / caution
If the raw p clears your cutoff but the adjusted p does not, the honest call at that cutoff is not significant; falling back to the raw p because you like it better defeats the point of correcting.
Bonferroni, Holm, and BH adjusted values cannot be below their raw p values. Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett may differ from a separate raw-test p because they use different statistics or error models.
Glossary

significant at .05

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Corr, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
A result is called significant at .05 when p is below .05, meaning the data would be fairly surprising if there were no real effect. It is a convention for flagging results worth a second look, not a verdict.
At alpha = .05, a result is called statistically significant when its relevant p value is below .05. This means the result crossed a chosen decision threshold; it does not prove that the finding is true.
How to read it
If p is below the cutoff the tool flags the result; at or above it, no flag. The cutoff defaults to .05, and panels with an Alpha control let you change it.
Plot Studio flags p < alpha and does not flag p >= alpha. Choose alpha as part of the analysis plan, considering the costs of false positives and false negatives.
Common misread / caution
Significant does not mean the effect is large, important, or definitely real, and a non-significant result is not proof that there is no effect (absence of evidence is not evidence of absence).
Statistical significance does not establish a large, important, replicable, or causal effect. Non-significance does not establish equivalence or absence; those claims require suitable interval or equivalence methods.
Glossary

sample size

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Dist, Corr, Likert, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Sample size is how many observations you have. Capital N usually means the whole sample, and lowercase n usually means one group or cell within it.
Sample size is the number of non-missing units contributing to an estimate or test. The unit might be a person, trial, school, or something else. Capital N often means the full analysis sample and lowercase n a group or cell, but notation varies.
How to read it
Check n per group, not only the total: small cells give shaky estimates and wide error bars, and very uneven group sizes are worth noting.
Check what the unit is, how missing values were handled, and the n for each result. Different correlations can use different cases, and some repeated-measures tests require complete subjects.
Common misread / caution
A big N does not make a finding true or important; it mainly makes small effects easier to detect, so even a tiny effect can turn significant with enough data.
Rows are not always independent units. Repeated or clustered observations may provide less independent information than the raw row count suggests. A large N improves precision but does not make an effect important, unbiased, or causal.
Glossary

95% confidence interval

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist, Corr, Likert, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
A 95% confidence interval is a range built to capture the true value 95% of the time if you repeated the study many times. As a working read, treat it as a plausible range for the true value given your data.
A 95% confidence interval comes from a method designed, under its assumptions, to capture the true population value in 95% of repeated samples. The interval from this dataset is one result from that method.
How to read it
Read the width as your uncertainty: narrow means a precise estimate, wide means a fuzzy one. The same logic applies at other levels (a 99% interval is wider than a 95% one from the same data). For a difference or a correlation, an interval that does not include zero points to an effect in that direction.
Its width shows precision under the model: narrower intervals are more precise. For a two-sided test using the same method, an interval that excludes 0 corresponds to rejecting a zero effect at alpha = .05; that does not make the effect important.
Common misread / caution
It is not correct to say there is a 95% probability the true value lies in THIS particular interval; the 95% describes how often the method captures the true value across many repeats, not this one interval.
The 95% is not the probability that the fixed population value lies inside this one interval, and the interval does not usually contain 95% of individual observations.
Glossary

multiple comparisons

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Corr

Before
After
Definition
When you run many tests at once, each one carries its own small chance of a false alarm, and those chances add up, so somewhere in the batch a fluke can look significant. Correcting for multiple comparisons keeps that batch-wide false-alarm rate in check.
When several tests are run, each true null hypothesis brings a chance of a false positive, so the chance of at least one false positive is usually larger than the alpha for one test. Multiple-testing methods control a chosen error rate across a defined family, such as the chance of any false positive or the expected false-discovery proportion.
How to read it
The more pairs or tests you run, the more you should lean on the adjusted p column rather than raw p.
Decide which tests belong to the family from the scientific question, then use the adjusted p values or decisions from the chosen method.
Common misread / caution
Do not read one lucky significant result out of many tests as a confirmed finding; with enough tests, some will cross .05 by chance alone.
The risks from separate tests do not simply add; their sum is only an upper limit. A correction also cannot make an undisclosed, data-driven search into a planned confirmatory analysis.
Glossary

Cohen's dz (paired)

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
For paired or repeated measures, Cohen's dz is the average change per person divided by the standard deviation of those change scores. It describes the size of the within-person difference in standard deviation units.
For paired or repeated measurements, Cohen's dz is the mean of the paired differences divided by the SD of those differences.
How to read it
Read it as the typical change measured against how much people vary in their change: larger absolute values mean a bigger, more consistent shift across the two occasions.
The sign shows the direction of the mean difference. A larger absolute value means the mean difference is larger relative to how much the paired differences vary.
Common misread / caution
Because dz is scaled by the variability of the change scores, it is not directly comparable to a two-group d, so do not treat the same 0.2, 0.5, 0.8 cutoffs as identical across the two.
It does not describe a typical person's change and is not directly interchangeable with independent-group d. Fixed small/medium/large labels are context-dependent.
Glossary

Hedges' g

1 field changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Common misread / caution
Not present
Several Hedges-g conventions and correction formulas exist. Report the software and denominator convention when exact cross-study comparability matters.
Glossary

rank-biserial r

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
Rank-biserial r is the effect size that goes with rank-based tests (like Mann-Whitney or Wilcoxon), summarizing how consistently one group's scores outrank the other's (or, for paired tests, how consistently the within-person changes lean one way). It ranges from -1 to +1.
A rank-based effect size from -1 to +1. For Mann-Whitney, it summarizes whether observations from one independent group tend to outrank those from the other. For Wilcoxon, it compares the positive and negative ranked paired differences.
How to read it
Read the absolute value as how one-sided the comparison is: near 0 means the groups overlap heavily, and near 1 (positive or negative) means one group's scores almost always outrank the other's.
Values near 0 show little overall rank advantage; values nearer -1 or +1 show that the ranked evidence falls mostly in one direction. Check group order before interpreting the sign: Plot Studio follows jamovi's convention, which can point opposite to Cohen's d.
Common misread / caution
It shares the letter r with Pearson correlation, but it is a rank-based measure of separation; do not read it as the correlation between two measured variables.
It is not Pearson correlation, and it does not by itself estimate a difference in means or medians.
Glossary

eta-squared

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
Eta-squared is the proportion of the total variation in the outcome that is associated with a factor in an ANOVA. It runs from 0 to 1 and is often read as a percentage of variance.
Plot Studio calculates eta-squared for an ANOVA term by dividing that term's sum of squares by the total corrected sum of squares. In a one-way ANOVA, it is the usual share of the sample's total variation associated with group differences.
How to read it
Read it as a share of the outcome's spread: 0.10 means about 10 percent of the total variation lines up with that factor.
For example, .10 means that the term's sum of squares is 10% of the total corrected sum of squares under this ANOVA calculation.
Common misread / caution
With more than one factor, every term's eta-squared is a share of the same TOTAL variation, all competing for the same 100 percent, so each value often looks smaller than its partial eta-squared.
In a factorial Type III ANOVA, adjusted terms can overlap, so their eta-squared values do not have to add to 1. Eta-squared also tends to overestimate the corresponding population effect.
Glossary

Partial eta-squared

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
Partial eta-squared is a factor's share of just the variation it could account for: its own effect plus the leftover error variation, with the other factors set aside. It runs from 0 to 1 and is usually larger than plain eta-squared for the same factor.
Partial eta-squared compares the variation linked to one ANOVA term with that term's variation plus its own error variation. Repeated-measures terms use the error term defined by their design.
How to read it
Read it as the factor's slice of the effect-plus-error variation for that term, not a slice of the whole outcome, which is why it usually runs larger than plain eta-squared.
It runs from 0 to 1. Read it as the term's variation divided by that variation plus its own error,not as a share of all outcome variation.
Common misread / caution
Partial eta-squared values do not sum to 1 across factors and are not comparable to plain eta-squared, so do not read them as slices of the same whole.
Values across terms do not add to 1 and depend on the design and error denominator. Do not compare them as though they were slices of one common whole.
Glossary

Omega-squared

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
Omega-squared estimates the proportion of variance a factor explains in the wider population, with an adjustment that makes it less optimistic than eta-squared. It is usually a bit smaller than eta-squared for the same data.
Omega-squared estimates the share of population variance associated with an ANOVA effect while correcting some of eta-squared's upward bias. It is often smaller than eta-squared, and an estimate can be zero or negative.
How to read it
Read it like eta-squared, as a share of variance, but treat it as the more conservative estimate of the true effect in the population. It can dip to zero or slightly below when the effect is about what noise alone would produce; read that as an effect near zero.
Treat a small negative value as an estimate near zero, not as negative variance. Formula conventions differ across designs, so report the software or method when comparing studies.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Bias correction does not make omega-squared assumption-free; it inherits the ANOVA model and design assumptions.
Glossary

Welch's t-test

2 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A test for whether two groups differ on their averages that does not assume the two groups have equal spread. It is the safer default for comparing two independent groups, since real groups often vary in how spread out they are.
A test of whether the population means of two independent groups differ. It allows the two populations to have different variances, or amounts of spread, and is generally the safer default when equal variance is uncertain.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Welch still assumes independent observational units and a meaningful mean with an adequate normal or large-sample approximation. It does not fix clustering, dependence, severe data errors, or selection bias.
Glossary

Student's t-test

2 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A test for whether two independent groups differ on their averages that assumes both groups have equal spread. Welch's version is usually preferred because it does not lean on that equal-spread assumption.
A test of whether the population means of two independent groups differ. It pools the group variances, so it assumes that the two populations have equal variance, or similar spread. Welch's version is usually preferred when that assumption is doubtful.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It assumes independent observational units, equal population variances, and the usual normal or large-sample approximation. Equal-looking sample SDs do not prove equal population variances.
Glossary

paired t-test

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
A test for whether the average change is zero when the same people (or matched pairs) are measured twice, such as before and after. It works on each person's difference score, which removes steady person-to-person differences.
A test of whether the population's average paired difference is zero,for example, when the same people are measured before and after. It works on each matched pair's difference score.
How to read it
t is built from the average difference divided by the noise in those differences; further from 0 means a more consistent change, and the p value tells you how surprising that change would be under no real change.
t is the mean difference divided by its SE. The sign shows direction; report the mean difference, confidence interval, and p value rather than t alone.
Common misread / caution
A significant paired test says a change this big would be surprising if the true average change were zero; it does not say the change is large, or that it happened for everyone.
Pairs must be correctly matched and independent of other pairs, and the difference-score mean needs an adequate normal or large-sample approximation. Significance does not mean every unit changed or that change was large.
Glossary

Mann-Whitney U

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A rank-based test for whether two independent groups differ, comparing their ranked positions instead of their means. It is a common choice when the data are ordinal or the distribution is skewed.
A test for two independent groups that works with ranked values rather than means. In general, it asks whether values from the two groups tend to occupy different ranks; it is not automatically a test of medians.
How to read it
You mainly read the p value; U itself just summarizes how the two groups' ranks stack up against each other.
Use the p value to assess the rank-distribution difference and report both groups' distributions plus a rank-based effect size. If the group distributions have similar shapes, the result can be interpreted as a location shift; if their shapes differ, the test can respond to those differences too.
Common misread / caution
It compares whole distributions through ranks, so it is not strictly a test of means, and a small p does not tell you the size of the gap.
Skewness alone does not make Mann-Whitney preferable to Welch's t-test. Different shapes can produce significance even when medians are equal.
Glossary

Wilcoxon signed-rank

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
A rank-based test for whether the typical difference is zero when the same people are measured twice. It is the rank-based counterpart to the paired t-test, useful when difference scores are skewed or ordinal.
A paired test that ranks the sizes of nonzero differences, then compares ranks attached to positive and negative differences. Its usual null assumes the difference distribution is symmetric around zero; under that assumption, it tests whether the distribution is centered at zero,technically, whether its pseudomedian is zero.
How to read it
You mainly read the p value; the V statistic summarizes the ranked sizes of the positive versus negative differences.
Plot Studio reports V as the sum of positive ranks. Use its p value to assess the signed-rank imbalance, and also report the paired differences and a rank-based effect size.
Common misread / caution
A significant result says the differences tend to lean one direction, not that the change is large or true for every person.
The test uses both signs and ranked magnitudes, so it is not assumption-free and is not generally justified by saying the scores are merely ordinal or skewed.
Glossary

the U statistic

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
The statistic behind the Mann-Whitney test, counting how often values in one group outrank values in the other. It is the input to the p value rather than a number you interpret directly.
A Mann-Whitney statistic equivalent to counting pairwise orderings between groups, with tied pairs contributing half. Plot Studio displays the smaller of the two complementary U values, while rank-biserial sign follows jamovi's first-sample convention.
How to read it
You rarely read U on its own; let the p value do the talking, and describe the groups with medians or ranks.
Interpret U through the group sizes and its null distribution; report the group distributions and rank-biserial effect.
Common misread / caution
Not present
U is not itself a difference in means or medians, and software may display U for a particular group rather than the smaller U.
Glossary

the V statistic

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
The statistic behind the Wilcoxon signed-rank test: rank the sizes of all the within-person differences, then add up the ranks of those in one direction. It is the input to the p value, not a stand-alone result.
Plot Studio removes zero paired differences, ranks the absolute nonzero differences, and reports V as the sum of ranks attached to positive differences, matching R's convention.
How to read it
You rarely read V on its own; look at the p value and summarize the differences with a median.
Interpret V through its null distribution and p value; report the paired-difference distribution and rank-biserial effect size.
Common misread / caution
Not present
V is not a median and different software can report a different signed-rank sum, so always name the convention when comparing output.
Glossary

Omnibus test

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
An omnibus test asks one broad question: is there evidence that at least one group or condition differs somewhere? It can say the overall pattern would be surprising if nothing differed, but not which groups differ.
An omnibus test asks one broad question about several values at once,for example, whether all group means are equal. A significant result says the joint null does not fit the data well under the model, but not which group or contrast differs.
How to read it
A small p is evidence that at least one group stands out; follow it up with specific pairwise comparisons to find out where.
Use planned contrasts or follow-up comparisons that account for multiple testing to locate and estimate the differences.
Common misread / caution
A significant omnibus result does not tell you which pair differs, so do not read it as a claim about any specific two groups.
A non-significant omnibus test does not prove all groups equivalent, and a significant one does not license every pairwise claim.
Glossary

One-way ANOVA

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A test that compares the means of the groups defined by a single grouping variable, typically three or more (with exactly two it matches a pooled t-test). It checks whether the spread between group means is larger than you would expect from ordinary variation within the groups.
One-way ANOVA compares the population means of groups defined by one categorical variable. It asks whether differences among group means are large relative to variation within groups, using one pooled error variance. With two groups, Plot Studio's F equals Student's t squared.
How to read it
Look at F and its p value: a small p means the group means differ more than ordinary within-group variation alone would produce.
Read F with its degrees of freedom and p value, then use effect estimates and planned or multiplicity-aware comparisons to describe where differences lie.
Common misread / caution
A small p is evidence the means are not all equal; it does not say which specific groups differ, or by how much.
The standard test assumes independent observational units, a common error variance, and normal errors for exact small-sample inference. It does not show which means differ or whether differences are important.
Glossary

Two-way ANOVA

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A test that looks at two grouping variables at once, reporting a main effect for each one plus their interaction. It lets you see each factor's effect and whether the factors combine in a way that is more than the sum of their parts.
Two-way ANOVA models an outcome using two categorical factors. It tests an overall main effect for each factor and an interaction,whether one factor's pattern changes across levels of the other.
How to read it
You get three rows: one main effect per factor and one interaction, each with its own F and p; read the interaction first, because it changes how you interpret the main effects.
Look at the interaction before summarizing the main effects, because an effect that changes across the other factor's levels can make an overall average incomplete or misleading.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Interaction depends on scale: a pattern can be additive on one transformed outcome scale and interactive on another.
Glossary

Type III sums of squares

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A bookkeeping method for splitting up variation when factors are unbalanced, so each effect is judged after accounting for all the others. This is the convention jamovi uses, and it matters mainly when group sizes are unequal.
Type III sums of squares test each ANOVA term after accounting for all the other terms, including interactions. Plot Studio uses sum-to-zero coding and matches jamovi's convention when every combination of factor levels has data.
How to read it
You do not read these numbers directly; they are the raw material the F values are built from.
The resulting sum of squares is used in that term's F statistic. Its meaning depends on the contrast coding and the interactions included in the model.
Common misread / caution
This is an accounting choice, not a result, so there is nothing here to interpret as a finding.
Type III term sums of squares are adjusted quantities, not mutually exclusive pieces that must add to total SS. Empty cells make some hypotheses non-estimable; Plot Studio refuses that factorial omnibus.
Glossary

Main effect

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
The overall effect of one factor, averaging across the levels of the other factor. It answers whether that factor makes a difference on its own.
A main effect compares one factor's model-based averages after averaging over the levels of the other factor. It asks whether those marginal means differ; it does not mean the factor acts 'by itself.'
How to read it
A small p for a factor is evidence its levels differ when you average over the other factor.
A small p is evidence that those modeled averages are not all equal.
Common misread / caution
When a strong interaction is present, a main effect can be misleading because the factor's effect is not the same across the other factor's levels.
A main effect is not a factor acting 'by itself.' Under interaction, averaging can conceal reversals or changing effects, so inspect cell means and simple effects.
Glossary

F statistic

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
A ratio comparing the variation an effect explains (differences among group or condition means) to the leftover error variation. Larger F values mean those differences are big relative to the noise.
F compares the variation represented by an ANOVA term with the error variation used for that term. Larger F values mean the term is large relative to the model's noise.
How to read it
F sits with its degrees of freedom and p value; a large F paired with a small p points to real differences among the groups, but the size of F is not itself an effect size.
Interpret F with both degrees of freedom and its p value. A large F with a small p is evidence against the relevant null under the model, not proof of a real or important difference.
Common misread / caution
A big F signals detectability, not importance; use an effect size to judge how large the difference actually is.
F is not an effect size and different ANOVA terms can use different denominators, especially in repeated-measures designs.
Glossary

One-way repeated-measures ANOVA

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
A test for when the same people are measured across several occasions or conditions (with exactly two it matches the paired t-test). It compares the occasion means while accounting for the fact that each person's scores are linked.
A test of condition means when the same subjects are measured in every displayed condition. Plot Studio uses subjects with complete data for all conditions and applies the Greenhouse-Geisser correction; with two conditions, F equals paired t squared.
How to read it
Read F and its p value as usual; the degrees of freedom may be fractional because of the Greenhouse-Geisser correction.
A small p is evidence that the condition means are not all equal. Use paired follow-up comparisons with a multiple-testing adjustment to find and estimate the differences.
Common misread / caution
A small p is evidence the occasion means are not all equal; it does not say which occasions differ, so follow up with pairwise comparisons.
Subjects must be independent of one another and observations correctly paired. Greenhouse-Geisser addresses sphericity, not outliers, missing-data bias, carryover, or dependence between subjects.
Glossary

Greenhouse-Geisser correction

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
An adjustment that protects a repeated-measures ANOVA when the sphericity assumption is shaky; this tool applies it automatically. It shrinks the degrees of freedom using a value called epsilon, estimated from your data, so the test does not flag differences too easily; when sphericity holds, epsilon is near 1 and almost nothing changes.
A correction for repeated-measures ANOVA when the sphericity assumption may not hold. Plot Studio estimates a number called epsilon and uses it to reduce both degrees of freedom before calculating the p value.
How to read it
Epsilon runs from a lower bound up to 1: values near 1 mean little correction was needed, and lower values mean a larger adjustment; the corrected degrees of freedom shrink accordingly.
For k repeated conditions, epsilon lies between 1/(k - 1) and 1. Values below 1 produce a more conservative test; epsilon = 1 leaves the degrees of freedom unchanged.
Common misread / caution
Epsilon is an assumption-check adjustment, not a result, so there is nothing to interpret in it as a finding.
Epsilon is an estimated correction factor, not a test result or an effect size. Automatic correction does not repair other design or model problems.
Glossary

Cousineau-Morey within-subject error correction

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
An adjustment to the error bars on a repeated-measures chart so they reflect within-person variation instead of raw between-person spread. It makes the bars match what the study actually compares: how each person changes across conditions.
An error-bar method for repeated-measures data. Plot Studio first removes each subject's overall level across the displayed conditions, then applies Morey's correction before computing SD, SE, or a confidence interval. The plotted mean itself does not change.
How to read it
These corrected bars are usually tighter than uncorrected ones; read them as showing the consistency of the changes within people, not the raw spread across people.
The bars reduce stable person-to-person differences and summarize normalized condition-level spread or uncertainty. They can be wider or narrower than ordinary bars.
Common misread / caution
These bars are built for comparing conditions within subjects, so do not read them as ordinary between-group error bars.
These are not error bars for a particular pairwise difference, not a measure of how reliably each person changed, and not suitable for comparing independent groups. Bar overlap is not a general significance test.
Glossary

None (no correction)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Each comparison is tested on its own at the usual cutoff (often .05), with no adjustment for how many tests you ran. When you make many comparisons, some will cross the line by chance alone, so this option lets more false positives through.
With no correction, each test keeps its own p value and alpha without accounting for the rest of the family. If several null hypotheses are true, the chance of at least one false positive is usually greater than the per-test alpha.
How to read it
Read each p value against your usual cutoff, but remember that with many tests, some small p values are expected even if nothing real is going on.
Use unadjusted results only when the tests genuinely stand alone under the analysis plan, or when controlling a family-level error rate is not the goal.
Common misread / caution
No correction does not mean the tests are more accurate or honest; it means the risk of a false positive piles up as you add comparisons.
Calling results exploratory does not make the accumulated false-positive risk disappear; disclose the number and selection of tests.
Glossary

Bonferroni

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Corr

Before
After
Definition
A strict adjustment that guards against getting any false positive across the whole family of tests by making each individual test harder to pass. It works by effectively dividing your cutoff by the number of comparisons.
Bonferroni limits the chance of one or more false positives across a chosen family to no more than alpha. It does this by multiplying each p value by the number of tests, up to 1, which is equivalent to dividing alpha by that number. The guarantee does not require independent tests.
How to read it
The reported p values are already adjusted, so compare them to your usual cutoff (like .05) as normal; a value that clears it has passed the stricter bar.
Compare the adjusted p values with the original alpha and report what counted as the family.
Common misread / caution
Bonferroni being strict does not mean a comparison that fails is truly null; it can hide real differences because it trades away sensitivity for tight false-positive control.
It controls an error probability; it does not guarantee zero false positives. Its conservatism can reduce power, especially with many dependent tests.
Glossary

Holm

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Corr

Before
After
Definition
A step-down method that controls the same overall false-positive risk as Bonferroni but adjusts the tests in order of their p values, so it is a bit less harsh. It gives the same protection while letting slightly more true differences through.
Holm first orders the p values, then applies a step-down version of Bonferroni. It controls the same family-wise error rate even when tests are dependent and is never less powerful than ordinary Bonferroni.
How to read it
The p values shown are already adjusted; compare each to your usual cutoff as you normally would.
Compare Holm-adjusted p values directly with alpha and report the family.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Greater power than Bonferroni does not guarantee that every additional rejection is true; the procedure controls a long-run family error rate.
Glossary

Benjamini-Hochberg (FDR)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq, Corr

Before
After
Definition
Instead of preventing any false positive, this controls the false discovery rate: the expected share of your flagged results that are actually false alarms. It is more forgiving than Bonferroni or Holm, which suits situations with many comparisons.
Benjamini-Hochberg controls the expected proportion of false positives among the hypotheses it rejects, rather than the chance of any false positive. This guarantee holds for independent tests and for common forms of positive dependence.
How to read it
The adjusted p values are compared to your usual cutoff; a value under it means the finding fits within your accepted rate of false discoveries.
Compare BH-adjusted p values with the chosen FDR level, and state which tests formed the family. BH often finds more results than family-wise methods because it controls a different error rate.
Common misread / caution
FDR does not promise this specific result is real; it manages the expected proportion of mistakes among all the results you flag, not the status of any one of them.
It does not promise that this particular finding is true or that the realized false share in this one analysis is below the target. Arbitrary dependence requires additional justification or another procedure.
Glossary

Tukey (studentized range)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A correction built for comparing every group mean against every other group mean at once, judging each gap against how big the largest gap would get by chance alone. It assumes roughly equal variances and is the standard follow-up to a one-way ANOVA.
Tukey's method compares every pair of independent-group means while controlling the family-wise false-positive rate for the whole set. It uses the pooled one-way-ANOVA error variance and assumes normal errors with equal population variances.
How to read it
The adjusted p values already account for all the pairwise comparisons, so read each against your usual cutoff.
Compare its family-adjusted p values directly with alpha. Plot Studio calculates them from the pooled ANOVA error term rather than by multiplying separate Welch p values.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Unequal variances or a family that is not all pairwise means calls for a different procedure; Tukey does not apply to rank or paired tests in Plot Studio.
Glossary

Games-Howell

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Definition
A version of the all-pairs comparison for when the groups do not have equal variances or equal sizes. Use it in place of Tukey when the spread differs a lot from group to group.
Games-Howell compares every pair of independent-group means while allowing the groups to have different variances and sample sizes. It uses separate variance estimates and adjusts the p values for the full set of pairwise comparisons.
How to read it
Read each adjusted p value against your usual cutoff; the adjustment already accounts for the unequal spreads and the multiple comparisons.
Compare each adjusted p value directly with alpha. The adjustment accounts for both unequal variances and multiple comparisons.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It still assumes independent units and a suitable mean-based sampling model. In Plot Studio it does not apply to paired or rank-based tests.
Glossary

Dunnett (vs control)

1 field changed

Modules: Compare

Before
After
Common misread / caution
Dunnett only speaks to treatment-versus-control differences; it says nothing about whether two treatment groups differ from each other.
It assumes independent units and a defensible common-variance normal linear model. It says nothing about treatment-versus-treatment contrasts and is not an unequal-variance alternative.
Glossary

Median

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
The median is the middle value when the data are sorted: half the values fall below it and half above. With an even count it is the average of the two middle values.
The median is the middle of the sorted data. At least half the observations are at or below it, and at least half are at or above it. When there is an even number of observations, Plot Studio averages the two middle values.
How to read it
Read it as the typical score that splits the group in half; if the median and mean are far apart, that is usually a sign of skew or extreme values.
Read it as a center that is less affected than the mean by a few very high or very low values.
Common misread / caution
The median is not the average, and it barely moves when a few values are extreme, which is exactly why it can differ from the mean.
With tied values, it is not always true that exactly half are strictly below and half strictly above. A gap between the mean and median can suggest an uneven shape, but it does not prove the distribution is skewed.
Glossary

SD (standard deviation)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
The standard deviation measures spread: roughly the typical distance of individual values from their mean. Larger SD means the scores are more spread out.
The sample standard deviation, or SD, measures how spread out the observations are around their mean, in the same units as the data. Plot Studio uses the common sample formula based on n - 1.
How to read it
Read it in the same units as the data; a small SD means the group is tight and consistent, a large SD means it is variable.
A larger SD means the observations are more spread out. A few extreme values can make SD much larger.
Common misread / caution
SD describes the spread of the raw data, not the precision of the mean; do not confuse it with the standard error, which is usually smaller.
SD describes variation in the observed values, not how precisely the mean has been estimated. In Repeated Measures, a within-subject SD is based on Cousineau-Morey normalized values, so it does not represent the raw differences between people.
Glossary

SE (standard error of the mean)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
The standard error estimates how much the sample mean would bounce around if you repeated the study many times. It is the SD divided by the square root of the sample size, so it shrinks as the sample grows.
The standard error estimates how much the sample mean would bounce around if you repeated the study many times. For an ordinary independent cell it is the SD divided by the square root of n. Repeated Measures can instead use a selected within-subject or between-subject method; the table footnote identifies it.
How to read it
Read it as the wobble in the mean itself; a smaller SE (often shown as a shorter error bar) means the mean is estimated more precisely.
Read it as the wobble in the mean itself; a smaller SE (often shown as a shorter error bar) means the mean is estimated more precisely. In Repeated Measures, use the method named below the table.
Common misread / caution
SE is about the precision of the mean, not the spread of the people; a small SE does not mean the scores are similar, only that the average is pinned down well.
SE is about the precision of the mean, not the spread of the people. In Repeated Measures it is not necessarily the ordinary SD divided by the square root of n.
Glossary

Skewness

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Skewness measures how lopsided a distribution is. A positive value means a longer tail stretching to the right (high values), a negative value means a longer tail to the left, and a value near zero means roughly symmetric.
Skewness summarizes asymmetry in a distribution. Positive skewness usually reflects a longer or more influential right tail, and negative skewness a longer or more influential left tail. A symmetric distribution has skewness 0, but a value near 0 does not by itself prove symmetry.
How to read it
Read the sign for the tail direction and the size for how lopsided: values past about 1 or below about -1 signal noticeable skew, but treat that as a rough guide, not a cutoff.
Use the sign as a guide to direction and the magnitude as a rough guide to strength. Values above about 1 or below about -1 are sometimes treated as notably skewed, but this is a convention, not a cutoff.
Common misread / caution
Skewness describes the shape of the tails, not whether the data are wrong or unusable; mild skew is common and often fine.
Skewness does not by itself say that the data are wrong or unusable. Mild asymmetry is common, and very different shapes can have similar skewness values.
Glossary

Kurtosis (excess kurtosis)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Excess kurtosis measures how heavy the tails are compared with a normal curve, which is set at zero here. A positive value means heavier tails and more outliers than normal, a negative value means lighter tails.
Plot Studio reports sample-adjusted excess kurtosis, a measure that gives extra weight to observations far from the mean. A normal distribution has excess kurtosis 0. Positive values indicate greater tail extremity than normal; negative values indicate less.
How to read it
Read it relative to zero: zero matches a normal curve, positive means more extreme values than normal, negative means fewer.
Compare it with 0, but inspect the graph too: even one extreme observation can change kurtosis substantially.
Common misread / caution
Kurtosis is about tail weight and outliers, not about how tall or pointy the peak looks, and it does not measure skew.
Kurtosis is not simply the height or pointiness of the center. A positive value does not guarantee that this particular sample contains more outliers.
Glossary

Shapiro-Wilk W

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
The Shapiro-Wilk test checks how closely a group's data match a normal distribution. W ranges up toward 1 (a perfect normal match), and its p value is small when the data depart from normal.
The Shapiro-Wilk test checks whether the pattern of ordered observations is consistent with a normal distribution. W is usually closer to 1 when the sample is more consistent with normality, but its meaning also depends on sample size. Plot Studio uses R's shapiro.test for 3 to 5,000 non-missing values.
How to read it
Read W as a closeness-to-normal score (nearer 1 is closer) and the p value as the flag: a small p (below .05) flags a departure from normal, a larger p means no departure was detected.
A small p is evidence against the normal model under the test. A larger p means the test did not detect a departure.
Common misread / caution
A large p does not prove the data are normal, it only means no departure was detected, and in small samples the test can miss real non-normality; a tiny p in a huge sample can flag a departure too small to matter.
W is not a percentage or a stand-alone closeness score. Small samples have low power and large samples can detect inconsequential departures, so inspect the Q-Q plot and the analysis's sensitivity to non-normality.
Glossary

Chi-square test of independence

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Definition
Tests whether two categorical variables are related, by asking if the counts in the table differ from what you would expect if the variables had no connection. A small p value says the pattern of counts would be surprising if the two variables were truly unrelated.
The chi-square test of independence asks whether two categorical variables are associated. It compares the observed cell counts with the counts expected if the variables were independent. Plot Studio reports Pearson's uncorrected chi-square test and its large-sample p value, including for 2 x 2 tables.
How to read it
Report the chi-square value with its df and p; a p below your alpha (often .05) means the counts depart from independence more than sampling noise easily explains.
A small p value means the observed pattern would be unusual if the variables were independent. Report chi-square, degrees of freedom, p, N, expected-count checks, and Cramer's V.
Common misread / caution
A significant result is evidence the variables are related, but says nothing about how strongly; check Cramer's V for that. And p is not the probability that the variables are unrelated.
The approximation assumes independent observational units and adequate expected counts, and the categories within each variable should be mutually exclusive. A significant result does not show that the association is strong or causal.
Glossary

the chi-square statistic

1 field changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Common misread / caution
A large chi-square is not automatically a large or important effect; it grows with sample size, so pair it with an effect size like Cramer's V or Cohen's w.
Chi-square generally grows with both discrepancy and sample size, so it is not an effect-size measure. Pair it with Cramer's V or Cohen's w.
Glossary

expected count

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Definition
The count a cell would hold on average if the null were true: for the independence test it is computed from the row and column totals, and for the goodness-of-fit test by splitting the total equally across the categories. The test works by comparing each observed count to this expected count.
An expected count is how many observations a cell would contain on average if the null hypothesis were true. For independence, Plot Studio calculates it as row total times column total divided by N. For its goodness-of-fit test, it divides N equally across the categories.
How to read it
These are the baseline the observed counts are measured against; the panel also shows the smallest expected count, because the test gets unreliable when any expected count is very low (a common rule of thumb is to worry below 5).
Compare observed and expected counts. Also check the smallest expected count, because very small expected counts can make the chi-square p value inaccurate.
Common misread / caution
Expected counts are a bookkeeping baseline, not a finding; do not interpret them as a result on their own.
The familiar cutoff of 5 is only a rule of thumb. Accuracy depends on how many cells have small expected counts and how small they are. Plot Studio does not automatically switch to an exact or simulated test.
Glossary

standardized residual

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Definition
For one cell, how far its observed count sits above or below its expected count, on a standardized scale. These are adjusted standardized residuals (Haberman), so they behave roughly like z-scores, which is what lets you compare cells and use a rule of thumb.
For each cell, Plot Studio's adjusted standardized, or Haberman, residual shows how far the observed count is above or below the expected count after scaling for that cell's sampling variation. With sufficiently large expected counts, it behaves roughly like a standard normal z score under independence.
How to read it
Read the sign and size: positive means more cases than expected, negative means fewer, and because they act like z-scores, a magnitude of about 2 or more marks a cell that departs notably.
Positive means more cases than expected and negative means fewer. Absolute values around 2 or larger can help locate cells that contribute strongly to the overall departure.
Common misread / caution
A large residual flags where the departure is, not that that single cell was run as its own significance test.
A table contains many residuals. Treating every value beyond +/-1.96 as a separate unadjusted test increases false positives, so use residuals as diagnostic clues alongside the overall test and multiplicity-aware follow-up.
Glossary

goodness-of-fit test

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Definition
Tests whether the counts across the categories of one variable match a set of expected proportions; here, an even split across the categories. A small p value says the observed spread of counts would be surprising if that split held.
The goodness-of-fit test asks whether one categorical variable follows an expected distribution. In Plot Studio, that expected distribution is an equal split across the k observed categories. Pearson's chi-square test then uses k - 1 degrees of freedom and a large-sample p value.
How to read it
Report chi-square with its df and p; a low p means at least one category holds more or fewer cases than the expected proportions predict.
A small p value is evidence against an equal population split. Report chi-square, degrees of freedom, p, N, expected counts, and Cohen's w.
Common misread / caution
It tells you the counts differ from the expected split, not which category differs or by how much; the standardized residuals show where.
The overall test does not identify which categories differ. Small expected counts can make the approximation inaccurate, and an equal split may be the wrong scientific null if another distribution was expected.
Glossary

two-proportion z-test

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Definition
Compares two proportions to ask whether they differ by more than sampling noise, for example the proportion saying yes in group A versus group B. A small p value says a gap this large would be surprising if the two true proportions were equal. (When both proportions come from the same sample, the tool uses a matched version of the test and the readout says so.)
This test asks whether two population proportions are equal. For independent groups, Plot Studio uses the pooled standard error required by the null hypothesis. When the two proportions are category shares from the same sample, it uses a variance that accounts for their dependence and identifies that version in the results.
How to read it
Report z with its p; a low p means the two proportions differ more than chance alone comfortably explains, and the sign of z tells you which proportion is larger.
The signed z value is the observed difference in proportions divided by its null standard error. The p value describes how unusual a difference at least this large would be if the population proportions were equal and the large-sample approximation were adequate.
Common misread / caution
Significance says the proportions differ, not by how much; read the difference in proportions to see the actual size of the gap.
Small counts can make the normal approximation poor. Statistical significance does not give the gap's magnitude, so report the signed difference and an appropriate confidence interval when available.
Glossary

% of Total

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq, Dist

Before
After
Definition
For each category or interval, the share of cases that fall in it: its count divided by the total, shown as a percent. It rescales raw counts so categories are comparable regardless of overall sample size.
A displayed percent is a count divided by the total used by the current chart, then multiplied by 100. In Frequencies, the usual denominator is the visible total within each panel. For 100% filled bars, it is the visible total within each category. In Distribution histograms, each group's proportion bars are normalized separately.
How to read it
Read each value as this slice's portion of the whole; a full set of categories adds to about 100 (rounding can nudge it). When the chart is split into panels or groups, the percents are figured within each panel or group, not across the whole dataset.
Name the denominator when reading the value: percent of the panel, percent within a category, or percent within a distribution group. Only the matching complete set sums to about 100%, apart from rounding.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Hidden frequency bars are excluded from the displayed denominator, even though the Statistics panel's raw-count table retains full-data counts. A displayed percent can therefore change when a bar is hidden.
Glossary

Cumulative %

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq, Dist

Before
After
Definition
A running total of the percentages taken in a set order of categories or intervals, so each row shows the share of cases at or before that point in the order. It reaches 100 once every row has been counted.
Cumulative % is a running total in a defined order. Distribution bins always accumulate from the lowest interval upward; sorting that table by count or percent changes only the displayed row order, not the calculation. Frequency categories accumulate in chart order within each panel.
How to read it
Read a row as the percent of all cases falling in it or anything earlier in the order; the order matters, so check which end the accumulation starts from.
Read it as the percentage accumulated through this category or interval in the underlying order and within the stated denominator.
Common misread / caution
Cumulative % depends entirely on the sort order of the rows, so a value only means what you think if you know how the categories are arranged.
A visually last row need not show 100 after a table is re-sorted, because sorting display rows does not change the underlying accumulation order.
Glossary

Pearson r

1 field changed

Modules: Scatter, Corr

Before
After
Common misread / caution
A near-zero r means no straight-line relationship, not that the variables are unrelated, since a curved pattern can hide behind a small r.
r is sensitive to outliers and range restriction, can miss nonlinear dependence, and does not establish causation. Its p value and Fisher interval require independent observational units and their sampling assumptions.
Glossary

Spearman rho

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter, Corr

Before
After
Definition
Spearman rho is a rank-based correlation: it ranks each variable from low to high and then measures how well the two sets of ranks track each other, from -1 to +1. Because it uses ranks, it captures any consistent increasing or decreasing pattern, not only a straight line, and it is less swayed by outliers.
Spearman rho is Pearson correlation applied to the variables' ranks. It measures monotonic association from -1 to +1: whether larger values of one variable tend to accompany consistently larger or consistently smaller values of the other.
How to read it
Read it like Pearson r (sign for direction, distance from 0 for strength), but remember it is describing the order of the values, not their exact spacing.
Read sign as direction and magnitude as monotonic strength; unlike Pearson r, exact spacing between values does not matter after ranking.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Ranks reduce sensitivity to extreme magnitudes but do not make rho immune to influential observations, ties, restricted ranges, or non-monotonic relationships.
Glossary

Fisher-z confidence interval

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
This is the confidence interval for a correlation, built using a math trick (the Fisher-z transform) that makes the interval behave properly near -1 and +1 where correlations get lopsided. It gives a plausible range for the true correlation in the population, not just the one r from your sample.
This interval gives a range of population correlations calculated from the sample. For Pearson r with at least four complete pairs, Plot Studio transforms r to Fisher z, builds the interval there, and converts the endpoints back to the -1 to +1 correlation scale.
How to read it
Read the two endpoints as the low and high edges of a plausible range for the true correlation; a wide interval means more uncertainty, and an interval that stays entirely above or below 0 signals that 0 is not a plausible value at this confidence level.
If this method were repeated across many similar samples under its assumptions, intervals at the stated level would contain the fixed population correlation that often. A narrower interval means a more precise estimate. A matching two-sided interval that excludes 0 agrees with rejecting a zero correlation at the corresponding alpha level.
Common misread / caution
The 95% refers to how often this procedure captures the true value across many samples, not a 95% probability that the true correlation sits inside this particular interval.
It does not mean there is a stated probability that the fixed population correlation lies inside this one interval. Coverage can be inaccurate with very small samples or when the assumptions behind Pearson correlation fit poorly.
Glossary

Strongest pair

3 fields changed

Modules: Corr

Before
After
Definition
In a correlation matrix, the strongest pair is the two variables with the largest correlation by magnitude, meaning the value furthest from 0 whether positive or negative. It flags where the tightest linear relationship in your set sits so you know where to look first.
The pair with the largest absolute value of the currently selected matrix coefficient: Pearson r, Spearman rho, or Kendall tau-b. It is chosen only from pairs with computable coefficients in the displayed variable set.
How to read it
Read it as the standout relationship in the matrix; check its sign to see whether that pair rises together or moves in opposite directions.
Check the selected method and sign. 'Strongest' is a relative ranking within this matrix, not a claim of significance, practical importance, stability, or causation.
Common misread / caution
Strongest means largest in size, so a strong negative correlation can be the strongest pair even though it is a negative number.
Because the app searches across several pairs and highlights the largest sample coefficient, that winner will often look stronger than it would in new data. Treat it as a lead to investigate, not a confirmed result.
Glossary

Cronbach's alpha

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
Cronbach's alpha summarizes internal consistency: how closely a set of items meant to measure the same thing move together, taking into account both the average relationship among the items and how many items there are. It is often between 0 and 1 (and can go negative when items work against each other); values around .70 and up are a common rule of thumb for being consistent enough, though that is a convention, not a hard cutoff.
Cronbach's alpha summarizes how a set of scored items hangs together in this sample. It is calculated from the item variances, their covariances, and the number of items. Plot Studio uses respondents with complete data on all items after any reverse scoring.
How to read it
Read it as higher means the items agree with each other more; very low alpha suggests the items are not measuring one shared thing, and very high alpha can mean some items are near-duplicates.
Report alpha with the number of items, sample size, scoring decisions, and missing-data handling. Interpreting alpha as reliability also assumes, among other things, that items contribute equally to the common score and that their errors are uncorrelated.
Common misread / caution
Alpha only tells you the items hang together, not that they measure the right construct (validity) or that they measure a single thing (unidimensionality).
There is no universal .70 pass mark, and alpha can be negative. Alpha does not by itself show that a scale is one-dimensional, valid, stable over time, or equally reliable for every subgroup or score level.
Glossary

Reverse-scored item

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
A reverse-scored item is worded in the opposite direction from the rest of the scale, so its response numbers are flipped before analysis to point the same way as the others. The (R) marker shows which items have been flipped so their high and low ends line up with the scale.
Reverse scoring flips an item's numeric direction so that high and low scores line up with the intended scale direction. For a shared discrete scale with k response levels, Plot Studio changes x to k + 1 - x before calculating percentages, means, and alpha. It does not apply this operation to variables handled as continuous because they have many distinct levels.
How to read it
When you see (R) next to an item, its scores have already been reversed, so read it in the same direction as every other item on the scale.
The (R) marker means that the discrete item has been reflected. Reverse an item only when its intended meaning and coding require it.
Common misread / caution
Forgetting to reverse a reverse-worded item leaves it fighting the others and can drag Cronbach's alpha down for a scale that is actually fine.
A negatively worded item is not automatically reverse-keyed. Reversal also cannot repair ambiguous wording, multiple dimensions, or a poorly specified scale.
Glossary

Internal consistency

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
Internal consistency is the degree to which the items on one intended scale give agreeing answers, so that a person who scores high on one item tends to score high on the others. It is the specific kind of reliability that Cronbach's alpha estimates.
Internal consistency describes how responses to items intended for one combined score relate to one another in a sample. Cronbach's alpha is one common summary. Consistency does not mean that people answer every item identically, and it becomes a reliability claim only under a suitable measurement model.
How to read it
Read higher internal consistency as: these items are telling a reasonably consistent story.
Before interpreting one coefficient, check the item content, scoring direction, dimensionality, and pattern of relationships among the items.
Common misread / caution
Consistent items are not automatically valid or measuring a single trait: internal consistency is about agreement among items, not about what they measure.
Strong item relationships can come from redundancy, shared wording, method effects, or several correlated dimensions. They do not by themselves prove one dimension or construct validity.
Glossary

Silverman's rule of thumb (bandwidth)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Bandwidth controls how much a density curve is smoothed. Plot Studio first chooses an automatic width using Silverman's rule of thumb: h = 0.9 times min(SD, IQR/1.34) times n^(-1/5). If IQR is 0 it uses SD, and if there is no usable spread it uses width 1. The Bandwidth control multiplies this automatic value.
How to read it
Not present
Below 1 reduces smoothing and above 1 increases it. Inspect whether important features persist across reasonable nearby settings.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The slider value is not the bandwidth in data units, and this rule is not optimal for every distribution, especially multimodal, bounded, or very small samples.
Glossary

Kernel (kernel density estimate)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A kernel density estimate places a small smooth bump at every observation and adds the bumps together. The Kernel setting chooses the bump's shape, while Bandwidth controls its width. Bandwidth usually changes the visible curve more.
How to read it
Not present
Try reasonable nearby bandwidths and, when useful, different kernels. Features that remain visible across those choices are more stable than bumps that appear under only one setting.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The curve is an estimate, not a uniquely determined outline of the population. Near a hard boundary such as zero, it can spill into impossible values, and small samples can produce unstable bumps.
Glossary

Density (as a statistic / y-axis)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
As a Statistic choice, Density rescales a histogram so the total area under the bars equals 1 (a probability density) instead of showing raw counts; with a Group By variable, each group's own bars are scaled to area 1 separately. It lets you compare distributions of different sample sizes on the same footing.
How to read it
Not present
Read the y-axis as density, not as a count or percent. A bin's fraction is its height times its width, and all bar areas, not all bar heights, add to 1.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A density value like 0.04 is not a count or a percent, it only means something once you multiply by the bin width.
Glossary

Gaussian kernel

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The Gaussian kernel is an unbounded normal-shaped weighting function centered at each observation. Every observation contributes some weight at every x, although distant contributions quickly become tiny.
How to read it
Not present
It produces a smooth estimate and is a common default. With comparable effective bandwidths, kernel shape usually matters less than bandwidth.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Gaussian refers to the smoothing kernel, not an assumption that the data or population are normally distributed.
Glossary

Epanechnikov kernel

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The Epanechnikov kernel uses a rounded, parabolic bump. An observation affects the curve only within a limited distance set by the bandwidth; beyond that distance, its weight is zero.
How to read it
Not present
It often produces a curve similar to other kernels. Bandwidth usually changes the result more than the choice between Epanechnikov and Gaussian bumps.
Common misread / caution
Not present
This kernel has a theoretical efficiency advantage under particular large-sample conditions, but that does not guarantee the best estimate for a particular dataset, especially near boundaries.
Glossary

Rectangular kernel

2 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The rectangular (uniform) kernel is a boxcar bump: every point within a cutoff distance set by the bandwidth counts equally, and points beyond it count not at all. It produces a blockier, step-like density.
How to read it
Not present
Read the rougher look as a feature of the flat bump shape, not as extra detail in the data.
Glossary

Normal curve overlay

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The normal overlay is a bell-shaped curve fitted separately to each displayed group using that group's sample mean and SD. Plot Studio rescales it to match Count, Density, or Proportion bars. It is a visual reference, not proof that the data are normal.
How to read it
Not present
Compare systematic shape differences such as skew, tail mismatch, or multiple modes, while remembering that binning and sample variability affect the appearance.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Neither visual agreement, Shapiro-Wilk, nor a Q-Q plot can confirm that a population is exactly normal. Use them together to assess whether departures matter for the intended analysis.
Glossary

LOESS (locally weighted smoothing)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
LOESS is a fit that follows the data's local trend by smoothing over nearby points, instead of forcing one straight or polynomial line through everything. It is useful when the relationship bends in ways a single equation would miss.
How to read it
Not present
Read the LOESS curve as a flexible summary of where y tends to sit as x changes, its wiggle is controlled by the Span.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It is a description of this sample's shape, not a tidy equation or a law you can extrapolate beyond the data.
Glossary

LOESS span

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Span controls the size of the neighborhood used for each local LOESS fit. The control runs from 0.1 to 1.5, with 0.75 as the default. Smaller spans use fewer nearby observations and produce more local wiggle. Larger spans use broader neighborhoods; above 1, all observations contribute and their distance weights become more similar, producing stronger smoothing.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as a smoothness dial: turn it down to chase local detail, turn it up to see the broad trend.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A very small span can trace noise as if it were signal, a smoother-looking curve is not automatically a better one.
Glossary

OLS (ordinary least squares)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Ordinary least squares is the standard method for drawing a straight best-fit line. It picks the line that makes the total of the squared vertical gaps between the points and the line as small as possible.
How to read it
Not present
Read the OLS line as the single straight trend that best balances all the points, its slope is the average change in y per one-unit change in x.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Because it squares the gaps, a few far-out points can pull the line noticeably, so check for outliers before trusting the slope.
Glossary

Polynomial fit (quadratic / cubic)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A polynomial regression models the mean outcome with powers of x. A quadratic can have at most one turning point and a cubic at most two, although either may show fewer within the observed x range.
How to read it
Not present
Read the displayed curve only over the data range and use uncertainty and subject-matter reasoning to judge whether its curvature is credible.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A cubic is not necessarily S-shaped, and adding degree can fit sample noise or behave wildly when extrapolated.
Glossary

Fit line (line of best fit)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A fit line is a modeled trend line that summarizes the overall relationship between x and y. It is a statistical estimate with assumptions behind it, not a line drawn through the actual points.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as the model's best guess at the average y for each x, and note the fit Type (linear, polynomial, LOESS) that produced it.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It is not a connect-the-dots path, and extending it past the range of your data is a guess, not a measurement.
Glossary

Confidence ellipse

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Although the control says Confidence ellipse, Plot Studio draws a model-based data ellipse, not a confidence region for the population mean. It uses the sample center and covariance with a bivariate-normal model. The stated level is the share of that fitted model's probability mass inside the ellipse.
How to read it
Not present
The tilt shows the direction of covariance, while the shape and size summarize spread. The ellipse is not forced to contain the stated percentage of observed points, so the percentage inside this sample will vary.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Coverage is approximate because center and covariance are estimated. Skewness, mixtures, heavy tails, or curvature can make the nominal level inaccurate.
Glossary

2-D density contours

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Contours are rings that map where points are densest, like a topographic map of the scatter. Each line marks an equal-density level, so tightly nested rings sit over the busiest part of the cloud.
How to read it
Not present
Nested contours identify regions of higher and lower estimated density. Closer spacing means the estimated density is changing more quickly across the plot; it does not by itself mean that a contour contains more observations.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The rings show where points pile up, not the boundary of the data, points can and do fall outside the outermost ring.
Glossary

Marginal distributions

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Marginals are the one-variable distributions of X and of Y, drawn along the edges of the scatter. They show how each axis is spread out on its own, ignoring the other variable.
How to read it
Not present
Read the top marginal as the shape of X alone and the side marginal as the shape of Y alone, useful for spotting skew or gaps a scatter can hide.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Two very different point clouds can share the same two marginals; the strips describe each variable alone, not the relationship between them.
Glossary

Confidence band

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
On a scatter fit, the ribbon contains a separate confidence interval for the estimated mean at each displayed x value. It is not a prediction interval for individual observations and does not guarantee that the entire curve is covered at once. The Q-Q envelope is different: it gives pointwise expected ranges for ordered sample quantiles under normality.
How to read it
Not present
For scatter fits, width describes uncertainty in the estimated mean curve at each x. For Q-Q plots, look for systematic departures rather than treating one escaped point as a stand-alone test.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Pointwise coverage at each location does not imply the whole line or all Q-Q points are simultaneously covered at that percentage.
Glossary

Bivariate Gaussian (normal) assumption

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The fitted data ellipse assumes that X and Y together form an approximately bivariate-normal distribution: roughly one oval-shaped population described by its center, variances, and covariance.
How to read it
Not present
Treat the ellipse as a model-based summary when the cloud is roughly one oval-shaped population.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Skewness, mixtures, heavy tails, outliers, or nonlinear structure can invalidate its nominal probability coverage. An oval-looking sample does not prove bivariate normality.
Glossary

Heatmap bin (2-D binning)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
In a scatter heatmap the plane is divided into a grid of tiles (bins), with the same number of bins running along each axis, and each tile is colored by how many points fall in it. This is 2-D binning: each bin covers a small patch of the x-y plane, not an interval of a single variable like a histogram bar.
How to read it
Not present
Read darker or hotter tiles as more crowded regions, the Bins control sets how fine the grid is.
Common misread / caution
Not present
These bins cover areas of the plane, not intervals of a single variable, so 'more bins' makes finer tiles, not narrower histogram bars.
Glossary

Standardized residual (regression)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Plot Studio takes each OLS residual (observed y minus fitted y) and divides it by the sample SD of that group's residuals. The calculation always uses the straight-line fit, even when a curved fit is displayed.
How to read it
Not present
Magnitude is vertical misfit in residual-SD units and is useful for screening unusual points.
Common misread / caution
Not present
This is a simple residual z-score, not a leverage-adjusted internally studentized residual. A large value does not by itself identify a data error or an influential observation.
Glossary

Q-Q plot (quantile-quantile)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A Q-Q plot is a normality check: it plots your sorted data against the positions a normal distribution would predict for the same ranks. If the data are roughly normal, the points fall close to a straight line.
How to read it
Not present
Read points hugging the reference line as roughly normal, and systematic curves or S-shapes as departures such as heavy tails or skew.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Small wiggles in the tails are normal for small samples, do not read every bend as a real violation.
Glossary

Theoretical quantile

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
On a Q-Q plot's x-axis, the theoretical quantile is where a standard normal distribution would place each ranked observation, a z-score-like position, so the axis runs in standard-deviation units rather than your data's units. It is the 'expected under a normal distribution' benchmark that your actual data get compared against.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as the ruler along the bottom: each point's x says where a normal distribution would have placed that ranked observation.
