Common misread / caution
Not present
Theoretical quantiles are model expectations, not any value from your dataset.
Glossary

Sample quantile

2 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
On a Q-Q plot's y-axis, the sample quantile is your actual observed data value, in sorted order, paired against the matching theoretical quantile. It is the real half of the comparison.
How to read it
Not present
Read each point as: a normal distribution expected this x, your data actually delivered this y.
Glossary

Reference line (Q-Q)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
On a Q-Q plot, the reference line shows where the points would fall if the data were normal. Plot Studio draws it through the first and third quartiles of your data (the classic qqline convention), so it is anchored to your data's own center and spread rather than being a fixed 45-degree diagonal.
How to read it
Not present
Read distance from the line as departure from normal: points drifting off the line at the ends signal heavy or light tails.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It is not the y = x diagonal, and its slope is not a finding: the slope simply tracks your data's spread. What matters is how closely the points follow the line, not the line's angle.
Glossary

Empirical CDF (ECDF)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The empirical CDF at x is the fraction of observations less than or equal to x. It is a right-continuous step function that jumps at each distinct observed value; ties make a larger single jump.
How to read it
Not present
Read straight up from x to obtain the observed fraction at or below it.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Plot Studio may average the two middle values when a sample has an even number of observations. That median can fall between observed values, so do not expect the ECDF step to identify it at exactly .5.
Glossary

Complement of the ECDF (1 - F)

2 fields changed

Modules: Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The complement flips the ECDF to show the fraction of data ABOVE each value instead of at-or-below (1 minus F). It is sometimes called a survival curve.
How to read it
Not present
Read straight up from any x to get the share of the data that exceeded it, the curve now falls from 1 to 0.
Glossary

Raincloud plot

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A raincloud plot combines three views: a half-violin density 'cloud' showing the shape, a box plot showing the summary, and the raw points ('rain') showing every observation. It packs distribution, summary, and data into one picture.
How to read it
Not present
Read the cloud for overall shape, the box for the median and quartiles, and the raw points for individual values and gaps.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Raw points can overlap, so one visible dot may represent more than one observation. Do not rely on the picture alone for the exact sample size.
Glossary

Pareto chart

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A Pareto chart combines count bars sorted from largest to smallest with a cumulative-percentage line. It shows how quickly the leading categories accumulate the total.
How to read it
Not present
Read bars as counts and the line as their cumulative share. An 80% reference can be useful for prioritization but is not a statistical cutoff.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The 80/20 pattern is not guaranteed and the sorted order discards any natural or temporal category order.
Glossary

Whisker (box plot)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
In a box plot, each whisker reaches from the box to the most extreme observed value that is still inside the 1.5 x IQR fence. Values beyond a fence are drawn separately. Plot Studio's violin inner whiskers are an exception: they extend to the observed minimum and maximum.
How to read it
Not present
Read a box-plot whisker as the reach of the observations not flagged by the fence rule. Its length depends on both the data and the rule, so it is not a direct measure of spread.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A box plot's whisker does not always reach the minimum or maximum, it stops at the last value inside the 1.5 x IQR fence. Only the violin's inner whiskers run to the extremes.
Glossary

Outlier (1.5 x IQR rule)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
On a box plot, Plot Studio flags an observation below Q1 - 1.5 x IQR or above Q3 + 1.5 x IQR and draws it as a separate point. The separate Outliers overlay can use a different multiplier or a standard-deviation rule.
How to read it
Not present
Treat a flagged point as a reason to inspect the value and its context. It is outside a chosen rule's boundary, not automatically an error.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Being flagged does not make a point an error or something to delete, it is a distance rule, not a verdict on the data.
Glossary

IQR (interquartile range)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The interquartile range is the spread of the middle 50% of the data: the third quartile (Q3) minus the first quartile (Q1). It is exactly the extent of the box in a box plot (its height on a vertical chart, its length on a horizontal one).
How to read it
Not present
Read the IQR as a robust measure of spread that ignores the extremes, a taller box means more variable middle-of-the-pack values.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The IQR describes the middle half only, it says nothing about how far the tails or outliers reach.
Glossary

Quartile / fence (Q1, Q3)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Q1 and Q3 mark the 25th and 75th percentiles, using R's type-7 calculation. The interquartile range, or IQR, is Q3 - Q1. The default box-plot fences are Q1 - 1.5 x IQR and Q3 + 1.5 x IQR.
How to read it
Not present
The box spans Q1 to Q3; whiskers end at the most extreme observed values inside the fences, and observations beyond them are flagged.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Quantile conventions can differ across software, especially in small samples. A flagged point is not automatically an error or a member of a different population.
Glossary

Error-bar type (SE / SD / CI)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Likert

Before
After
Definition
Not present
SD bars show how spread out the observed values are. SE bars show how precisely a mean is estimated. Confidence-interval bars extend an SE using the selected confidence level. These mean-based bars are not drawn for median summaries.
How to read it
Not present
Always state which type is shown. For repeated measures, also state whether the bars use within-subject Cousineau-Morey normalization or ordinary between-subject spread.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Overlap or non-overlap of separate error bars is not a general pairwise significance test. Use the appropriate comparison and its interval or p value.
Glossary

Two-tailed vs one-tailed test

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A two-sided test allows differences in either direction. A one-sided test is set up for one direction chosen before seeing the data. A result in that direction is evaluated in that tail; a result in the opposite direction receives a large one-sided p value.
How to read it
Not present
Use a one-sided test only when the direction was specified in advance and a result in the opposite direction would not support the claim. Plot Studio labels one-tailed results explicitly.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Choosing the direction after seeing the estimate or switching tails to cross alpha invalidates the advertised error rate.
Glossary

Significance bracket

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A significance bracket is an added bracket that spans two bars and labels the comparison between them. You can type the label yourself, or tick 'Auto-compute p-value from raw data' on its Stats tab to run a real test live and show the p-value, stars, or a full APA line. Drag each bracket leg onto a bar to pick the two groups.
How to read it
Not present
A bracket means that the two groups were selected for comparison. If its label was computed automatically, read the p value or stars using the stated test and alpha; a typed label is only an annotation.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A bracket reports only the pair it spans. No bracket between two groups does not show that they are equal, and a bracket by itself does not show a large or important effect.
Glossary

n.s. / significance asterisks

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
This is the star shorthand for p thresholds: * for p < .05, ** for p < .01, *** for p < .001, and 'n.s.' for not significant. It is a compact way to label a bracket without printing the exact p.
How to read it
Not present
Read more stars as a smaller p, and 'n.s.' as 'the difference did not reach the .05 cutoff.'
Common misread / caution
Not present
More stars mean a smaller p, not a bigger or more important effect, significance and effect size are different things.
Glossary

Confidence / coverage level (Level %)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist, Scatter, Likert

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The level is the long-run target for how often an interval method captures the fixed population value, assuming the method's model is correct. With the same data and method, a 99% interval is wider than a 90% interval because it uses a larger margin.
How to read it
Not present
The level describes the method across repeated samples; the interval's width shows the precision obtained from this dataset.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It is not the probability that the fixed value lies in this one interval. Except for displays specifically designed to cover data, it is also not the percentage of raw observations inside.
Glossary

Kendall tau (tau-b)

3 fields changed

Modules: Scatter, Corr

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Kendall's tau is a rank-based correlation from -1 to +1 that measures how often two variables' orderings agree. It compares every pair of cases: pairs ordered the same way on both variables (concordant) count for it, pairs ordered opposite ways (discordant) count against it. Plot Studio computes the tau-b variant, which adjusts for tied values, and it is an alternative to Pearson and Spearman.
How to read it
Not present
Read tau near +1 as 'when one goes up the other almost always does too,' near 0 as no consistent ordering, near -1 as reliably opposite.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Tau is usually smaller than Spearman's rho on the same data, that is a scale difference, not a weaker relationship.
Glossary

Diverging color scale

3 fields changed

Modules: Corr

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A diverging color scale uses two hues that meet at a neutral midpoint, here white at r = 0. It encodes both the sign and the strength of a correlation, unlike a one-hue sequential ramp that only shows magnitude.
How to read it
Not present
Read the hue for direction (one color for positive, the other for negative) and the depth of color for strength.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A pale cell means a correlation near zero, not missing data, and the two ends are opposite signs, not just 'more' and 'less.'
Glossary

Lower / upper triangle (correlation matrix)

3 fields changed

Modules: Corr

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A correlation matrix is symmetric: the cell for A-with-B equals the cell for B-with-A. So the lower and upper triangles mirror each other, and showing only one removes the redundancy.
How to read it
Not present
Read a single triangle as the full set of unique pairs, nothing is lost by hiding the mirror half.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The two triangles are not different results, they are the same correlations shown twice.
Glossary

Diagonal (correlation matrix)

3 fields changed

Modules: Corr

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Plot Studio displays 1 in every diagonal cell as the conventional self-correlation reference. Mathematically, a correlation is undefined for a variable with no variation, even though the display still uses 1 on its diagonal. Because the diagonal does not compare two different variables, it can be shown or hidden.
How to read it
Not present
Read a diagonal 1 as a display convention, not as a finding or as evidence that the variable can form valid correlations with other variables.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The perfect 1s on the diagonal are a definition, not a finding about your data.
Glossary

Significance stars (correlation matrix)

3 fields changed

Modules: Corr

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Stars use the fixed ladder * p < .05, ** p < .01, and *** p < .001, but Plot Studio suppresses a star whenever that p also fails the chart's Alpha setting. When Adjust p is on, both stars and cell treatment use the adjusted p.
How to read it
Not present
More stars means a smaller p within the fixed ladder. Read the exact p and Alpha setting for the full decision.
Common misread / caution
Not present
When Alpha is above .05, a p between .05 and Alpha can pass Alpha yet receive no star. Therefore no star does not always mean 'not significant at the selected Alpha.' Stars never indicate effect size or importance.
Glossary

Non-significant cells treatment

3 fields changed

Modules: Corr

Before
After
Definition
Not present
This setting controls how the matrix displays cells whose p value is not below Alpha, after any p adjustment. You can show them normally, fade them, blank them, or cross them out. The setting changes only the display.
How to read it
Not present
Read a faded, blank, or crossed cell as 'did not meet the selected significance threshold,' not as a zero or missing correlation.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A non-significant result does not prove that the population association is zero. It can reflect a small effect, an imprecise estimate, or both.
Glossary

Jitter

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Jitter nudges points sideways along the category axis with small random-looking offsets so overlapping values do not stack exactly on top of each other. The offsets are seeded from the data, so they stay put across redraws instead of reshuffling. It reveals how many points are hiding at each spot.
How to read it
Not present
Read the sideways scatter as a de-cluttering trick only: the offset carries no meaning, just the value-axis position does. On a repeated-measures chart with Connect Subjects on, each person keeps the same offset at every occasion so their line stays straight.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The sideways spread is arbitrary placement, not a second variable, so do not interpret a point's offset.
Glossary

Beeswarm

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A beeswarm layout moves points sideways just enough to reduce overlap while leaving their measured values unchanged. Points stay within the selected Spread width, so some may still overlap when space is tight. Regions with many nearby values usually form wider bulges.
How to read it
Not present
Use the bulges as a rough visual cue to where values are concentrated, not as an exact density estimate. Only the value-axis position is measured; the sideways position is used for packing.
Common misread / caution
Not present
As with jitter, the sideways position is only for packing, it is not a measured quantity.
Glossary

Strip plot

2 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A strip layout adds no sideways offset. All points in a category share the same category position, so identical or nearby values may overlap and hide how many observations are there.
How to read it
Not present
One visible dot may represent several observations. Switch to jitter or beeswarm when seeing overlap is important.
Glossary

Spread (jitter / swarm width)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Spread sets how far the jitter or beeswarm points may fan out on each side, as a fraction of the bar or category width. It is a layout setting, not a statistic, and the Strip and No-scatter modes ignore it.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as a crowding dial: widen it when points overlap, narrow it to keep each group compact.
Common misread / caution
Not present
This spread has nothing to do with statistical spread such as standard deviation, it only controls point placement.
Glossary

Rug marks (rug plot)

3 fields changed

Modules: Dist, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A rug draws one tick at each observation's axis position, adding raw-value locations beside a distribution or scatter display.
How to read it
Not present
Clusters of visible ticks suggest concentration and gaps suggest sparse regions.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Identical or very close values can overlap, so visible tick count and darkness do not reliably show how many observations share a position.
Glossary

Spaghetti plot / subject connectors

3 fields changed

Modules: RM

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Subject connectors draw one line per participant, joining that person's values across occasions. The tangle of lines (a spaghetti plot) lets you see individual trajectories, not just the group averages.
How to read it
Not present
Read each line as one person's path over time, look for lines that rise while others fall, which group means alone would hide.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A rising group average does not mean everyone rose, individual lines can head the opposite way.
Glossary

Facet / faceting (panels)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, Dist, Freq, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Faceting splits one chart into a row or grid of small panels, one per level of a variable, so you can compare patterns side by side. Each panel is the same chart drawn on just the rows belonging to that level. In Plot Studio the variable goes in the Panels box.
How to read it
Not present
Read across the panels to compare how the pattern changes from group to group, they share the same axes by default so heights are comparable.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Equal heights can be compared directly only while the panels share the same y-axis scale. With Free Y turned on, compare the axis numbers instead of the mark heights.
Glossary

Free Y axis (independent per-panel scales)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, Dist, Freq, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Free Y gives each facet panel its own y-axis range, based on the data in that panel. This can make patterns within a panel easier to see, but equal mark heights across panels no longer represent equal values.
How to read it
Not present
Read each panel using its own y-axis numbers. You can still compare numerical values across panels, but not their heights alone.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Different scales can make a small change in one panel look as tall as a much larger change in another. Always check the numerical ranges.
Glossary

Axis break (truncated axis)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
An axis break is the // mark that signals the value axis was cut and does not start at zero. It is a chart-integrity cue: the axis is truncated on purpose, and the mark discloses it.
How to read it
Not present
Read the // as a warning to check the axis numbers before judging how big the differences really are.
Common misread / caution
Not present
For bars, an axis break does not remove the distortion caused by a nonzero baseline because bar length encodes value. For lines, dots, and boxes, a nonzero axis can be appropriate, but the omitted range still needs to be shown clearly.
Glossary

Zero baseline

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The zero baseline is the principle that length-encoded charts like bars must start at zero. If a bar axis starts higher, the bar lengths exaggerate the differences between groups.
How to read it
Not present
Read bars only when they begin at zero, then a bar twice as long really means twice as much.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Position-encoded charts (lines, dots, box plots) do not need a zero baseline the way bars do, the rule is specific to length.
Glossary

Smooth / interpolation (line charts)

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Smooth draws a curved, interpolated path between the plotted points instead of straight line segments. It looks tidier but invents a shape for the space between your measured values.
How to read it
Not present
Read a smoothed line's peaks and dips between points as decoration, only the marked points are real data.
Common misread / caution
Not present
The curve passes exactly through your data points, but between them it can swing higher or lower than either neighbor. Do not read those in-between bulges as measurements; only the marked points were observed.
Glossary

Colorblind-safe / CVD-safe

3 fields changed

Modules: Compare, RM, Dist, Freq, Corr, Likert, Scatter

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Plot Studio's CB-safe badge means that the app simulated two forms of red-green color-vision deficiency, protanopia and deuteranopia, and that every color pair passed its built-in minimum-separation check.
How to read it
Not present
Treat the badge as one screening result. Also inspect the CVD, tritanopia, and grayscale previews, and keep labels, shapes, or other cues that do not depend on color alone.
Common misread / caution
Not present
No simulation can guarantee that every viewer will distinguish every color on every display or at every mark size. The badge lowers risk; it is not a universal accessibility guarantee.
Glossary

Diverging stacked bar

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
Not present
The diverging stacked bar is the default Likert chart: agree-side levels stack rightward and disagree-side levels stack leftward from a shared center line. The split at zero shows the balance of sentiment for each item.
How to read it
Not present
Every bar hangs on the same zero line, so read the balance: the farther a bar reaches right of zero, the more agreement on that item; the farther left, the more disagreement. Rank items by their right-side reach, or switch Sort items to Most agree to have the chart rank them for you.
Common misread / caution
Not present
A neutral middle category is split around zero, so the exact center line is a display convention, not a hard count boundary.
Glossary

100% stacked bar (normalized stacking)

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert, Freq

Before
After
Definition
Not present
A 100% stacked bar rescales every bar so its segments sum to 100%, showing each category's share within the bar rather than raw counts. It is built for comparing composition across bars of different totals.
How to read it
Not present
Read segment lengths as percentages of that bar. Every bar has the same total length, so compare composition rather than amount.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Normalization hides each bar's sample size: a narrow segment from a large group can represent more cases than a wide segment from a small group.
Glossary

Center / split-middle boundary

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
Not present
In a diverging bar, the zero line is a visual boundary from which the lower and upper response sides extend. On an odd-length scale, Plot Studio splits the single middle category equally across that line; an even-length scale has a boundary but no middle category.
How to read it
Not present
Treat zero as an alignment reference for comparing response balance, not as an observed response value.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Splitting a middle category is a display convention and does not classify half of those responses as favorable or unfavorable.
Glossary

Net-agreement sort

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
Not present
Net-agreement sort orders the Likert items by net agreement, the agree-side share minus the disagree-side share. It ranks items by overall favorability rather than by any single response column.
How to read it
Not present
With Most agree, read the top item as the most net-positive and the bottom as the most net-negative; Least agree flips that order. Dragging a row afterward switches the sort to Custom.
Common misread / caution
Not present
It sorts by the agree-minus-disagree balance, not by the count of any one level, so a strongly-mixed item can rank near neutral.
Glossary

Top-box (favorable percentage)

3 fields changed

Modules: Likert

Before
After
Definition
Not present
In Plot Studio, the favorable bar-end summary adds every response level on the agree side and excludes a neutral middle level; the unfavorable summary similarly adds the disagree side.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as the share selecting any favorable level under this chart's ordered scale and center boundary. State the included response levels when reporting it.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Industry terminology varies: 'top-box' can mean only the single highest category, while 'top-two-box' or favorable percentage combines several. Plot Studio's value is the full agree-side percentage, not necessarily the conventional single top box.
Glossary

Proportion (statistic)

3 fields changed

Modules: Freq, Dist

Before
After
Definition
Not present
As a Statistic choice, Proportion shows the 0-to-1 fraction form of a percent (0.42 instead of 42%). It is the same information as a percent, just on a different scale, and an alternative to raw Count.
How to read it
Not present
Read a proportion of .42 as 42% of the total, multiply by 100 to get the familiar percent.
Common misread / caution
Not present
Proportion and percent carry identical information, do not read the smaller-looking numbers as smaller quantities.
Linked definition

Test

1 field changed

Linked term key: test

Before
After
Definition
Which statistical test decided whether this pair differs. The right test depends on your data; Auto picks a sensible default.
The statistical test used to evaluate the comparison in this row. Different tests answer different questions and make different assumptions; Auto chooses a default that matches the design.
Linked definition

p value

2 fields changed

Linked term key: p

Before
After
Definition
The probability of a result at least as extreme as yours if there were really no effect. A small p means your data would be surprising in a world with no effect.
A p value asks: if the stated null hypothesis and the test's assumptions were true, how often would the test statistic be at least as extreme as the one observed? A smaller p means the data are less compatible with that null model.
How to read it
Read it against your cutoff (often .05); it is shown with no leading zero, so .03 means 0.03. It is not the probability that the effect is real.
Compare it with the alpha level chosen for the analysis; .03 means 0.03. It is not the probability that the null is true, that chance caused the result, or that an effect is real.
Linked definition

p adjusted

2 fields changed

Linked term key: pAdj

Before
After
Definition
A p value recalculated to account for running several tests at once, so a fluke somewhere in the batch is less likely to fool you. When you compare many pairs, use this column.
A p value that accounts for a family of several tests. Bonferroni, Holm, and BH adjust the raw p values. Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett instead calculate multiplicity-adjusted p values using their own comparison models.
How to read it
A stricter bar to clear: expect it to run higher than the raw p, and compare it to your cutoff the same way.
Compare it directly with alpha. Bonferroni, Holm, and BH adjusted values cannot be below their raw p values. Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett may differ from a separate raw-test p because they use different statistics or error models.
Linked definition

N

2 fields changed

Linked term key: nSize

Before
After
Definition
The number of observations (people) in that cell.
The number of non-missing units contributing to this row or cell. A unit might be a person, trial, school, or something else, depending on the study.
How to read it
Check n per cell, not only the total: small cells give shaky estimates and wide error bars.
Check what the unit is and look at n for each cell, not just the total. Repeated or clustered observations are related, so the raw number of rows may overstate how much independent information you have.
Linked definition

Median

2 fields changed

Linked term key: median

Before
After
Definition
The middle value when the cell's data are sorted: half fall below it and half above.
The 50th percentile of the sorted values: at least half the observations are at or below it and at least half are at or above it. With an even sample, Plot Studio averages the two middle values.
How to read it
If the median and mean are far apart, that is usually a sign of skew or extreme values.
Unlike the mean, the median is resistant to a small number of extreme values. Mean-median differences can suggest asymmetry, but do not diagnose it by themselves.
Linked definition

SD (standard deviation)

2 fields changed

Linked term key: sd

Before
After
Definition
Spread: roughly the typical distance of individual values from their mean. Larger SD means the scores are more spread out.
How spread out individual values are around their mean, in the same units as the data. A larger SD means the values are more spread out.
How to read it
SD is the spread of the data, not the precision of the mean; do not confuse it with SE.
SD describes the spread of the observations, not the precision of the estimated mean; do not confuse it with SE.
Linked definition

SE (standard error)

2 fields changed

Linked term key: se

Before
After
Definition
How much the cell's mean would wobble from sample to sample: the SD divided by the square root of n, so it shrinks as the sample grows.
How much the cell's mean would wobble from sample to sample. For an ordinary independent cell it is the SD divided by the square root of n. Repeated Measures instead uses the selected within-subject or between-subject method; the table footnote names which one.
How to read it
A smaller SE (a shorter error bar) means the mean is estimated more precisely.
A smaller SE (a shorter error bar) means the mean is estimated more precisely. In a repeated-measures chart, interpret it using the method named below the table.
Linked definition

Interaction

2 fields changed

Linked term key: interactionEffect

Before
After
Definition
Whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another. A significant interaction means the factors do not act independently: the pattern for one changes across the other.
An interaction means that the pattern for one factor changes across the levels of another. In ANOVA terms, the factors are not simply additive on the outcome scale being analyzed.
How to read it
Read the interaction first: when it is significant, describe each factor's effect within the levels of the other rather than overall.
A small interaction p is evidence that the modeled difference is not the same at every level of the other factor. Examine the cell means or simple effects instead of relying only on overall main effects.
Linked definition

Eta-squared

2 fields changed

Linked term key: etaSq

Before
After
Definition
The share of the total variation in the outcome associated with this factor, from 0 to 1.
For an ANOVA term, Plot Studio calculates eta-squared as that term's sum of squares divided by the total corrected sum of squares.
How to read it
With more than one factor, each eta-squared is a slice of the TOTAL variation, so the values can look small.
In a one-way ANOVA, this can be read as a share of the sample's total variation. In a factorial Type III ANOVA, the terms are adjusted and can overlap, so their eta-squared values do not have to add to 1.
Linked definition

Partial eta-squared

2 fields changed

Linked term key: etaSqP

Before
After
Definition
This factor's share of just the variation it could account for (its own effect plus the leftover error variation), with the other factors set aside. It is usually larger than plain eta-squared.
Partial eta-squared compares the variation linked to one ANOVA term with that term's variation plus its own error variation. Repeated-measures terms use the error term defined by their design.
How to read it
Partial eta-squared values do not sum to 1 across factors.
It runs from 0 to 1, but it is not a share of all outcome variation. Values for different terms do not add to 1 and can change with the design and error denominator.
Linked definition

Omega-squared

2 fields changed

Linked term key: omegaSq

Before
After
Definition
Like eta-squared but adjusted to be a less optimistic estimate of the effect in the wider population. It is usually a bit smaller than eta-squared.
Like eta-squared, omega-squared estimates how much population variation is associated with an ANOVA effect, but it corrects some of eta-squared's upward bias. It is often a little smaller.
How to read it
It can dip to zero or slightly below when the effect is about what noise alone would produce; read that as an effect near zero.
The estimate can be zero or negative. Read a small negative value as an effect near zero, not as negative variance.
Linked definition

Shapiro-Wilk W

2 fields changed

Linked term key: shapiroW

Before
After
Definition
The Shapiro-Wilk test checks how closely a group's data match a normal bell curve. W runs up toward 1 (a perfect match), and its p value is small when the data depart from normal.
The Shapiro-Wilk test compares the ordered sample with the pattern expected from a normal distribution. W is generally nearer 1 when the sample is more compatible with normality, but W is not a percentage or a stand-alone closeness score; its p value also depends on sample size.
How to read it
A small p (below .05) flags a departure from normal; a larger p just means none was detected, not proof of normality.
A small p flags evidence against normality under the test. A larger p means no departure was detected, not that normality was proved.
Linked definition

Kurtosis (excess)

2 fields changed

Linked term key: kurtosis

Before
After
Definition
How heavy the tails are compared with a normal curve, which is set at zero here. Positive means heavier tails and more outliers than normal; negative means lighter tails.
Excess kurtosis summarizes how strongly extreme values affect the distribution compared with a normal distribution, which is set to 0. Positive values mean greater tail extremity; negative values mean less.
How to read it
Read it relative to zero; it is about tail weight and outliers, not how tall or pointy the peak looks.
Sample kurtosis is strongly influenced by extreme observations. It does not measure how tall, flat, or pointy the center of the distribution looks.
Linked definition

Chi-square

2 fields changed

Linked term key: chiSq

Before
After
Definition
A test of whether the counts differ from what you would expect if the categories had no relationship (or matched a set of proportions). A small p means the pattern of counts would be surprising under that no-difference assumption.
Pearson's chi-square compares observed counts with the counts expected under the null hypothesis. Plot Studio uses the usual uncorrected statistic and an approximate chi-square p value for tests of independence or equal proportions.
How to read it
Read it with its df and p, and pair it with an effect size (Cramer's V or Cohen's w), because chi-square grows with sample size.
Interpret it only with df, p, expected counts, and an effect size. It assumes independent observational units and can be inaccurate with sparse expected counts.
Linked definition

% of Total

2 fields changed

Linked term key: percentTotal

Before
After
Definition
For each category or bin, the share of cases it holds: its count divided by the total, shown as a percent. When the chart is split into panels, the percents are figured within each panel.
A cell's count shown as a percentage of the total being used for that chart. Usually the total is all visible cases in the panel. In a 100% filled bar it is the total for that category, and in a grouped proportion histogram each group uses its own total.
How to read it
A full set of categories adds to about 100 within its panel; rounding can nudge it.
Check the denominator,the total used to calculate the percentage,before comparing values. Each complete set adds to about 100%, apart from rounding.
Linked definition

Cumulative %

2 fields changed

Linked term key: cumulativePct

Before
After
Definition
A running total of the percentages as you move down the rows, so each row shows the share of cases at or below that point.
A running percentage over categories or intervals in a defined order. Histogram bins accumulate from the lowest interval upward. Frequency categories accumulate in chart order within each panel.
How to read it
It depends on the row order, so check which end the accumulation starts from; it reaches 100 once every row has been counted.
Check both the order and the denominator. Sorting a histogram table only rearranges the already calculated rows, so a count-sorted view may not show 100% in its last visible row.
Linked definition

Pearson r

2 fields changed

Linked term key: pearsonR

Before
After
Definition
How strongly two numeric variables move together in a straight line, from -1 (perfect down) through 0 (no linear pattern) to +1 (perfect up). It captures straight-line patterns only.
A measure of straight-line association between two numeric variables, from -1 to +1. The sign gives direction and the distance from 0 gives strength; outliers and a restricted range can change it substantially.
How to read it
Sign is direction, distance from 0 is strength: near 0 weak, near -1 or +1 strong. A curved pattern can hide behind a small r.
A curved relationship can have a small r. Pearson r does not establish causation, and statistical tests for r assume independent units and an appropriate sampling model.
Linked definition

Spearman rho

2 fields changed

Linked term key: spearmanRho

Before
After
Definition
A rank-based correlation: it ranks each variable and measures how well the two sets of ranks track each other, from -1 to +1. It catches any steady up or down pattern and resists outliers.
Pearson correlation applied to the variables' ranks. It measures monotonic association from -1 to +1: whether larger values of one variable tend to accompany consistently larger or consistently smaller values of the other.
How to read it
Read it like Pearson r, but it describes the order of the values, not their exact spacing.
Read sign as direction and magnitude as monotonic strength. Ranks reduce sensitivity to extreme magnitudes, but Spearman rho is not immune to influential observations or unusual patterns.
Linked definition

Confidence interval

2 fields changed

Linked term key: ci95

Before
After
Definition
A range built to capture the true value most of the time if you repeated the study. As a working read, treat it as a plausible range for the true value given your data.
A confidence interval is a range calculated from the sample. If the same procedure were repeated many times under its assumptions, the stated percentage of those intervals would contain the fixed population value.
How to read it
Narrow means a precise estimate, wide means fuzzy. For a difference or correlation, an interval that excludes 0 points to an effect in that direction.
A narrower interval means a more precise estimate under the model. For a two-sided interval built to match a hypothesis test, excluding 0 corresponds to rejecting a zero effect at the matching alpha level. It does not show that the effect is practically important.
Linked definition

Strongest pair

2 fields changed

Linked term key: strongestPair

Before
After
Definition
In a correlation matrix, the two variables with the largest correlation by size (furthest from 0, positive or negative). It flags where the tightest linear relationship sits.
In a correlation matrix, the pair with the largest absolute value of the currently selected coefficient: Pearson r, Spearman rho, or Kendall tau-b.
How to read it
Check its sign: a strong negative correlation can be the strongest pair even though it is a negative number.
Check the method and sign. 'Strongest' is relative to the displayed set and does not imply causation, statistical significance, or practical importance.
Linked definition

Cronbach's alpha

2 fields changed

Linked term key: cronbachAlpha

Before
After
Definition
A summary of internal consistency: how closely a set of items meant to measure one thing move together, taking in both their average agreement and how many items there are.
A coefficient describing how a set of scored items hangs together in this sample. It is computed from item variances, covariances, and the number of items. Treating alpha as reliability requires assumptions, including that items contribute equally to the common score and that their errors are uncorrelated.
How to read it
Around .70 and up is a common rule of thumb for consistent enough; it can go negative when items work against each other. It is not a validity check.
Report alpha with the item count, sample size, scoring decisions, and missing-data handling. There is no universal .70 pass mark; alpha can be negative and does not by itself show one dimension or validity.
Linked definition

Welch's t-test

2 fields changed

Linked term key: welchT

Before
After
Definition
A test for whether two independent groups differ on their averages that does not assume the two groups have equal spread. It is the safer default, since real groups often vary in how spread out they are.
Welch's t-test compares the population means of two independent groups without assuming that their variances are equal. It uses the two group variances separately and Welch-Satterthwaite degrees of freedom. The observations still need to be independent and suitable for inference about means.
How to read it
A larger t (further from 0) means a bigger group difference relative to noise; check the p value and the difference in means to judge it.
The signed t value is the estimated mean difference divided by its standard error. Report the mean difference, its uncertainty, and p value. Welch's test does not fix dependent observations, serious data errors, or a poorly defined unit of analysis.
Linked definition

Student's t-test

2 fields changed

Linked term key: studentT

Before
After
Definition
A test for whether two independent groups differ on their averages that assumes both groups have equal spread. Welch's version is usually preferred because it does not lean on that assumption.
Student's t-test compares the population means of two independent groups. It combines the two group variances into one pooled estimate, so it assumes that the population variances are equal. It also relies on independent observations and the usual normal or large-sample approximation.
How to read it
Read t the same way as Welch: further from 0 means a bigger difference relative to noise.
Use it when the equal-variance model is defensible; otherwise Welch's test is generally safer. Report the mean difference and its uncertainty, not t alone.
Linked definition

Paired t-test

2 fields changed

Linked term key: pairedT

Before
After
Definition
A test for whether the average change is zero when the same people (or matched pairs) are measured twice, such as before and after. It works on each person's difference score, which removes steady person-to-person differences.
The paired t-test compares two measurements from the same people or matched units by first calculating each pair's difference. It tests whether the population mean difference is zero. Pairs must be correctly matched, different pairs must be independent, and the difference scores need the usual normal or large-sample approximation.
How to read it
A significant paired test says a change this big would be surprising if the true average change were zero, not that the change is large or happened for everyone.
Report the signed mean difference, its uncertainty, and p value. A significant result does not mean that every unit changed or that the change was large.
Linked definition

Mann-Whitney U

2 fields changed

Linked term key: mannWhitney

Before
After
Definition
A rank-based test for whether two independent groups differ, comparing their ranked positions instead of their means. A common choice when the data are ordinal or the distribution is skewed.
Mann-Whitney compares two independent groups by ranking their combined observations. In general, it tests whether values from one group tend to rank differently from values in the other; it is not automatically a test of medians. Only when the distributions have comparable shapes can it be read cleanly as a location shift.
How to read it
You mainly read the p value; a small p does not tell you the size of the gap, so report a median or an effect size too.
Use the p value for the rank-distribution comparison and report the group distributions with a rank-based effect size. Skewness alone does not make Mann-Whitney preferable to Welch's t-test.
Linked definition

Wilcoxon signed-rank

2 fields changed

Linked term key: wilcoxonSR

Before
After
Definition
A rank-based test for whether the typical difference is zero when the same people are measured twice. It is the rank-based counterpart to the paired t-test, for when difference scores are skewed or ordinal.
Wilcoxon signed-rank is a paired test that ranks the nonzero differences by size and keeps their signs. It tests whether the difference distribution is symmetric around zero. Under symmetry, this concerns the method's center, called the pseudomedian, rather than simply the ordinary sample median.
How to read it
You mainly read the p value; a significant result says the differences tend to lean one direction, not that the change is large.
A small p value indicates an imbalance between positive and negative signed ranks under that symmetry model. It does not show that every person changed or that the change was large.
Linked definition

U statistic

2 fields changed

Linked term key: uStat

Before
After
Definition
The statistic behind the Mann-Whitney test, counting how often values in one group outrank values in the other. It is the input to the p value, not a number you read directly.
The Mann-Whitney U statistic is based on pairwise ordering: each observation in one group is compared with each observation in the other, and tied pairs contribute half. Plot Studio displays the smaller of the two complementary U values. The sign of its effect size separately follows jamovi's first-sample convention.
How to read it
Let the p value do the talking, and describe the groups with medians or ranks.
U depends on the two sample sizes, so interpret it through its null distribution and p value. Report the group distributions and rank-biserial effect rather than treating U itself as a location difference.
Linked definition

V statistic

2 fields changed

Linked term key: vStat

Before
After
Definition
The statistic behind the Wilcoxon signed-rank test: rank the sizes of the within-person differences, then add up the ranks of those in one direction. It is the input to the p value, not a stand-alone result.
The Wilcoxon signed-rank statistic used here: zero differences are removed, absolute differences are ranked, and the ranks attached to positive differences are summed.
How to read it
Look at the p value and summarize the differences with a median.
Interpret V through its null distribution and p value. Describe the full distribution of paired differences and report the rank-biserial effect for size. The signed-rank test is not simply a test of the ordinary median.
Linked definition

Cohen's dz (paired)

2 fields changed

Linked term key: cohensDz

Before
After
Definition
For paired or repeated measures, the average change per person divided by the standard deviation of those change scores: the size of the within-person difference in standard deviation units.
For paired or repeated measurements, Cohen's dz is the sample mean of the pairwise differences divided by the sample SD of those differences. It expresses the average difference in difference-score SD units.
How to read it
Larger absolute values mean a bigger, more consistent shift across the two occasions. It is not directly comparable to a two-group d.
Larger absolute values mean that the mean difference is large relative to the variation among the pairwise differences. Its denominator differs from independent-group d, so the values are not directly interchangeable.
Linked definition

Rank-biserial r

2 fields changed

Linked term key: rankBiserialR

Before
After
Definition
The effect size that goes with rank-based tests (Mann-Whitney or Wilcoxon), summarizing how one group tends to score above the other using ranks rather than means. It ranges from -1 to +1.
A signed rank-based effect size from -1 to +1. For Mann-Whitney it summarizes pairwise ordering between groups; for Wilcoxon it compares the total positive and negative signed ranks. Plot Studio follows jamovi's sign convention, which can point opposite to Cohen's d for the same independent-group ordering.
How to read it
Near 0 means the groups overlap heavily; near 1 (either sign) means one group almost always outranks the other. It is not a Pearson correlation between two measured variables.
Values near -1 or +1 mean that nearly all pairwise comparisons or signed ranks point in one direction; values near 0 mean little net rank dominance. It is not Pearson correlation and does not by itself describe a median difference.
Linked definition

Reverse-scored item

2 fields changed

Linked term key: reverseScored

Before
After
Definition
An item worded in the opposite direction from the rest of the scale, so its response numbers are flipped before analysis to point the same way as the others.
Reverse scoring flips an item's numeric direction. For a shared discrete scale with k response levels, Plot Studio changes x to k + 1 - x before calculating percentages, means, and alpha. It does not reverse variables handled as continuous because they have many distinct levels.
How to read it
The (R) marker means its scores have already been reversed, so read it in the same direction as every other item. Forgetting to reverse such an item can drag Cronbach's alpha down for a scale that is actually fine.
The (R) marker means that the discrete item has been reflected. Reverse an item only when its intended meaning and coding require it; reversal cannot repair a multidimensional or poorly keyed scale.
Linked definition

Bandwidth (Silverman's rule of thumb)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: silverman

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Bandwidth (Silverman's rule of thumb)
Definition
Not present
Bandwidth controls how much a density curve is smoothed. Plot Studio chooses an automatic width using Silverman's rule of thumb: h = 0.9 times min(SD, IQR/1.34) times n^(-1/5). If IQR is 0 it uses SD, and if there is no usable spread it uses width 1. The slider multiplies that automatic value.
How to read it
Not present
A multiplier below 1 reduces smoothing and above 1 increases it. This normal-reference rule is a convenient default, not an optimal bandwidth for every distribution.
Linked definition

Kernel (density bump shape)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: kernelKde

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Kernel (density bump shape)
Definition
Not present
A kernel density estimate places a small smooth bump at every observation and adds the bumps together. Kernel chooses the bump's shape, while Bandwidth controls its width. Bandwidth usually changes the visible curve more.
How to read it
Not present
Treat the curve as an estimate whose details can change with kernel and bandwidth, not as a uniquely determined outline of the population.
Linked definition

Density (the histogram statistic)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: densityStat

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Density (the histogram statistic)
Definition
Not present
As a Statistic choice, Density rescales a histogram so the total area under the bars equals 1 (a probability density) instead of showing raw counts; with a Group By variable, each group's own bars are scaled to area 1 separately. It lets you compare distributions of different sample sizes on the same footing.
How to read it
Not present
Read the y-axis as density, not as a count or percent. A bin's fraction is its height times its width, and all bar areas, not all bar heights, add to 1.
Linked definition

Normal curve overlay

3 fields changed

Linked term key: normalCurve

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Normal curve overlay
Definition
Not present
The normal overlay is a bell-shaped reference curve fitted separately to each displayed group using that group's sample mean and SD. Plot Studio rescales it to match Count, Density, or Proportion bars.
How to read it
Not present
Use gaps between the bars and curve to look for skew, tail differences, or multiple peaks. Treat agreement as a visual check, not proof of normality, because binning and sample variability affect the appearance.
Linked definition

LOESS (locally weighted smoothing)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: loess

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
LOESS (locally weighted smoothing)
Definition
Not present
LOESS is a fit that follows the data's local trend by smoothing over nearby points, instead of forcing one straight or polynomial line through everything. It is useful when the relationship bends in ways a single equation would miss.
How to read it
Not present
Read the LOESS curve as a flexible summary of where y tends to sit as x changes, its wiggle is controlled by the Span.
Linked definition

LOESS span

3 fields changed

Linked term key: loessSpan

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
LOESS span
Definition
Not present
Span controls the size of the neighborhood used for each local LOESS fit. The control runs from 0.1 to 1.5, with 0.75 as the default. Smaller spans use fewer nearby observations and wiggle more. Larger spans use broader neighborhoods; above 1, all observations contribute and their distance weights become more similar.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as a smoothness dial: turn it down to chase local detail, turn it up to see the broad trend.
Linked definition

Fit line (regression)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: fitLine

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Fit line (regression)
Definition
Not present
A fit line is a modeled trend line that summarizes the overall relationship between x and y. It is a statistical estimate with assumptions behind it, not a line drawn through the actual points.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as the model's best guess at the average y for each x, and note the fit Type (linear, polynomial, LOESS) that produced it.
Linked definition

Confidence ellipse

3 fields changed

Linked term key: confEllipse

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Confidence ellipse
Definition
Not present
Although the control says Confidence ellipse, Plot Studio draws a model-based data ellipse, not a confidence region for the population mean. It uses the sample center and covariance with a bivariate-normal model. The stated level is the share of that fitted model's probability mass inside the ellipse.
How to read it
Not present
Use the tilt to read the direction of covariance and the shape and size to summarize spread. The ellipse is not forced to contain the stated percentage of observed points, and its coverage is approximate because its center and covariance are estimated.
Linked definition

Marginal distributions

3 fields changed

Linked term key: marginals

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Marginal distributions
Definition
Not present
Marginals are the one-variable distributions of X and of Y, drawn along the edges of the scatter. They show how each axis is spread out on its own, ignoring the other variable.
How to read it
Not present
Read the top marginal as the shape of X alone and the side marginal as the shape of Y alone, useful for spotting skew or gaps a scatter can hide.
Linked definition

Confidence band

3 fields changed

Linked term key: confBand

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Confidence band
Definition
Not present
On a scatter fit, the ribbon contains a separate confidence interval for the estimated mean at each displayed x value. It is not a prediction interval for individual observations and does not guarantee that the entire curve is covered at once. The Q-Q envelope instead gives pointwise expected ranges for ordered sample quantiles under normality.
How to read it
Not present
For scatter fits, read width as uncertainty in the estimated mean curve at each x. For Q-Q plots, systematic departures outside the pointwise envelope are evidence against the normal model.
Linked definition

Bivariate Gaussian (normal) assumption

3 fields changed

Linked term key: bivariateNormal

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Bivariate Gaussian (normal) assumption
Definition
Not present
The fitted data ellipse assumes that X and Y together form an approximately bivariate-normal distribution: roughly one oval-shaped population described by its center, variances, and covariance. Skewness, multiple clusters, heavy tails, outliers, or curvature can make the stated coverage inaccurate.
How to read it
Not present
Treat the ellipse as a model-based summary, not proof of bivariate normality and not a confidence region for the population mean.
Linked definition

Heatmap bin (2-D binning)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: heatmapBin

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Heatmap bin (2-D binning)
Definition
Not present
In a scatter heatmap the plane is divided into a grid of tiles (bins), with the same number of bins running along each axis, and each tile is colored by how many points fall in it. This is 2-D binning: each bin covers a small patch of the x-y plane, not an interval of a single variable like a histogram bar.
How to read it
Not present
Read darker or hotter tiles as more crowded regions, the Bins control sets how fine the grid is.
Linked definition

Standardized residual (regression)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: regrStdResidual

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Standardized residual (regression)
Definition
Not present
For each point, Plot Studio subtracts the y value predicted by the straight fitted line from the observed y value. This difference is the residual. It then divides the residual by the sample SD of the residuals in the same group. The calculation still uses the straight line when a curved fit is displayed. It is not the leverage-adjusted residual reported by some regression programs.
How to read it
Not present
A value of +2 means the point is about two residual SDs above the fitted line; -2 means it is about two residual SDs below. A large absolute value is a reason to inspect the point, not proof that it is wrong or influential.
Linked definition

Q-Q plot

3 fields changed

Linked term key: qqPlot

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Q-Q plot
Definition
Not present
A Q-Q plot is a normality check: it plots your sorted data against the positions a normal distribution would predict for the same ranks. If the data are roughly normal, the points fall close to a straight line.
How to read it
Not present
Read points hugging the reference line as roughly normal, and systematic curves or S-shapes as departures such as heavy tails or skew.
Linked definition

Q-Q reference line

3 fields changed

Linked term key: qqRefLine

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Q-Q reference line
Definition
Not present
On a Q-Q plot, the reference line shows where the points would fall if the data were normal. Plot Studio draws it through the first and third quartiles of your data (the classic qqline convention), so it is anchored to your data's own center and spread rather than being a fixed 45-degree diagonal.
How to read it
Not present
Read distance from the line as departure from normal: points drifting off the line at the ends signal heavy or light tails.
Linked definition

Empirical CDF (ECDF)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: ecdf

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Empirical CDF (ECDF)
Definition
Not present
The empirical CDF shows, at each x, the fraction of observations less than or equal to x. It is right-continuous and jumps at each distinct observed value; tied observations produce a larger single jump.
How to read it
Not present
Choose an x value and read up to the curve. A height of .70 means that 70% of the observations are at or below that value. Large or closely spaced jumps indicate many values in that region; flat sections contain no observed values.
Linked definition

Raincloud plot

3 fields changed

Linked term key: raincloud

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Raincloud plot
Definition
Not present
A raincloud plot combines three views: a half-violin density 'cloud' showing the shape, a box plot showing the summary, and the raw points ('rain') showing every observation. It packs distribution, summary, and data into one picture.
How to read it
Not present
Read the cloud for overall shape, the box for the median and quartiles, and the raw points for individual values and gaps. Points can overlap, so the exact sample size may not be countable from the picture alone.
Linked definition

Pareto chart

3 fields changed

Linked term key: pareto

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Pareto chart
Definition
Not present
A Pareto chart combines categories sorted from largest to smallest count with a cumulative-percentage line. It helps identify how quickly the leading categories accumulate the total.
How to read it
Not present
Read bars as counts and the line as their cumulative share. Eighty percent is a commonly drawn reference, not a statistical law and not evidence that an 80/20 pattern must exist.
Linked definition

Outlier (Tukey fence rule)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: outlierRule

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Outlier (Tukey fence rule)
Definition
Not present
On a box plot, Plot Studio flags a value below Q1 - 1.5 times the IQR or above Q3 + 1.5 times the IQR. These cutoffs are called Tukey fences. The separate Outliers overlay uses the same rule by default, but lets you change the multiplier or choose a standard-deviation rule.
How to read it
Not present
A flagged value is unusual under the selected rule and worth checking. It is not automatically a mistake, a member of a different population, or a value that should be removed.
Linked definition

IQR (interquartile range)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: iqr

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
IQR (interquartile range)
Definition
Not present
The interquartile range is the spread of the middle 50% of the data: the third quartile (Q3) minus the first quartile (Q1). It is exactly the extent of the box in a box plot (its height on a vertical chart, its length on a horizontal one).
How to read it
Not present
Because the IQR uses the middle 50% of the data, it is less affected by a few extreme values than the range or SD. A larger IQR means that the central half of the values is more spread out.
Linked definition

Quartiles and fences (Q1, Q3)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: quartileFence

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Quartiles and fences (Q1, Q3)
Definition
Not present
Plot Studio uses R's type-7 interpolated quantiles: Q1 is the estimated 25th percentile and Q3 the 75th. Tukey fences are Q1 - k times IQR and Q3 + k times IQR, with k = 1.5 by default.
How to read it
Not present
The box spans Q1 to Q3; whiskers end at the most extreme observed values inside the fences, and observations beyond them are flagged.
Linked definition

Error-bar type (SE, SD, CI)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: ebType

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Error-bar type (SE, SD, CI)
Definition
Not present
SD bars summarize observed spread around a mean; SE bars show the estimated sampling variability of the mean; confidence-interval bars apply a critical value to an SE for a stated long-run coverage procedure. Median summaries draw none of these mean-based bars.
How to read it
Not present
State which bar is shown. Bar overlap is not a general hypothesis test, and repeated-measures bars must be interpreted using the within- or between-subject method named by the table footnote.
Linked definition

Two-tailed vs one-tailed

3 fields changed

Linked term key: tails

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Two-tailed vs one-tailed
Definition
Not present
A two-sided test allows a difference in either direction. A one-sided test is set up for one direction chosen before seeing the results. A result in that direction is evaluated in that tail; a result in the opposite direction receives a large one-sided p value.
How to read it
Not present
Use a one-sided test only when the direction was specified in advance and a result in the opposite direction would not support the claim. Plot Studio labels every one-sided result explicitly.
Linked definition

Significance bracket

3 fields changed

Linked term key: sigBracket

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Significance bracket
Definition
Not present
A significance bracket is an added bracket that spans two bars and labels the comparison between them. You can type the label yourself, or tick 'Auto-compute p-value from raw data' on its Stats tab to run a real test live and show the p-value, stars, or a full APA line. Drag each bracket leg onto a bar to pick the two groups.
How to read it
Not present
Read the bracket as 'these two were compared,' and read its label (p or stars) for whether the difference reached significance. An auto-computed label recalculates whenever the data change.
Linked definition

Confidence level (coverage %)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: covLevel

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Confidence level (coverage %)
Definition
Not present
A 95% confidence level describes a method that, under its assumptions, would capture the fixed population value in about 95% of intervals from repeated samples. With the same data and method, choosing a higher level such as 99% makes the interval wider than choosing 90%.
How to read it
Not present
Do not read 95% as a 95% chance that this one interval contains the fixed population value, or as meaning that 95% of the raw observations lie inside it.
Linked definition

Diverging color scale

3 fields changed

Linked term key: divergingScale

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Diverging color scale
Definition
Not present
A diverging color scale uses two hues that meet at a neutral midpoint, here white at r = 0. It encodes both the sign and the strength of a correlation, unlike a one-hue sequential ramp that only shows magnitude.
How to read it
Not present
Read the hue for direction (one color for positive, the other for negative) and the depth of color for strength.
Linked definition

Non-significant cells

3 fields changed

Linked term key: nsTreatment

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Non-significant cells
Definition
Not present
This setting controls how the matrix displays cells whose p value is not below the selected Alpha level, after any p-value adjustment. You can show them normally, fade them, blank them, or cross them out.
How to read it
Not present
A faded, blank, or crossed cell means only that the result did not meet the selected significance threshold. It does not mean that the correlation is zero, negligible, or absent.
Linked definition

Jitter

3 fields changed

Linked term key: jitter

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Jitter
Definition
Not present
Jitter nudges points sideways along the category axis with small random-looking offsets so overlapping values do not stack exactly on top of each other. The offsets are seeded from the data, so they stay put across redraws instead of reshuffling. It reveals how many points are hiding at each spot.
How to read it
Not present
Read the sideways scatter as a de-cluttering trick only: the offset carries no meaning, just the value-axis position does. On a repeated-measures chart with Connect Subjects on, each person keeps the same offset at every occasion so their line stays straight.
Linked definition

Spread (point cloud width)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: spreadWidth

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Spread (point cloud width)
Definition
Not present
Spread sets how far the jitter or beeswarm points may fan out on each side, as a fraction of the bar or category width. It is a layout setting, not a statistic, and the Strip and No-scatter modes ignore it.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as a crowding dial: widen it when points overlap, narrow it to keep each group compact.
Linked definition

Rug marks

3 fields changed

Linked term key: rugMarks

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Rug marks
Definition
Not present
A rug adds a small tick at the axis position of every observation, showing where raw values occur beside a distribution or scatter plot.
How to read it
Not present
Clusters of ticks suggest concentrations and gaps suggest sparse ranges. Identical or nearby values can overlap, so the visible ticks do not always reveal how many observations are present.
Linked definition

Subject connectors (spaghetti lines)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: subjectConnectors

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Subject connectors (spaghetti lines)
Definition
Not present
Subject connectors draw one line per participant, joining that person's values across occasions. The tangle of lines (a spaghetti plot) lets you see individual trajectories, not just the group averages.
How to read it
Not present
Read each line as one person's path over time, look for lines that rise while others fall, which group means alone would hide.
Linked definition

Panels (faceting)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: facet

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Panels (faceting)
Definition
Not present
Faceting splits one chart into a row or grid of small panels, one per level of a variable, so you can compare patterns side by side. Each panel is the same chart drawn on just the rows belonging to that level. In Plot Studio the variable goes in the Panels box.
How to read it
Not present
Read across the panels to compare how the pattern changes from group to group, they share the same axes by default so heights are comparable.
Linked definition

Free Y axis (per-panel scales)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: freeY

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Free Y axis (per-panel scales)
Definition
Not present
Free Y lets each facet panel use its own y-axis range, based on the data in that panel. This can make patterns within a panel easier to see, but equal mark heights across panels no longer represent equal values.
How to read it
Not present
Check the y-axis numbers in every panel. You can still compare numerical values across panels, but you cannot compare their heights directly when the scales differ.
Linked definition

Axis break (//)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: axisBreak

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Axis break (//)
Definition
Not present
An axis break is the // mark that signals the value axis was cut and does not start at zero. It is a chart-integrity cue: the axis is truncated on purpose, and the mark discloses it.
How to read it
Not present
Read the // as a warning to check the axis numbers before judging how big the differences really are.
Linked definition

Zero baseline

3 fields changed

Linked term key: zeroBaseline

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Zero baseline
Definition
Not present
The zero baseline is the principle that length-encoded charts like bars must start at zero. If a bar axis starts higher, the bar lengths exaggerate the differences between groups.
How to read it
Not present
Read bars only when they begin at zero, then a bar twice as long really means twice as much.
Linked definition

Colorblind-safe (CVD-safe)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: cvdSafe

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Colorblind-safe (CVD-safe)
Definition
Not present
The CB-safe badge means that Plot Studio simulated two forms of red-green color-vision deficiency, protanopia and deuteranopia, and that every color pair passed the app's minimum-separation check. This lowers risk, but it cannot guarantee that every viewer will distinguish every color.
How to read it
Not present
Treat the badge as one screening result. Also inspect the CVD, tritanopia, and grayscale previews, and keep labels, shapes, or other cues that do not depend on color alone.
Linked definition

Center (split-middle boundary)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: centerSplit

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Center (split-middle boundary)
Definition
Not present
In a diverging Likert bar, the zero line is a visual boundary from which the lower and upper response sides extend. With an odd number of response levels, Plot Studio splits the single middle category equally across the line. With an even number of levels, there is a boundary but no middle category.
How to read it
Not present
Use zero as an alignment reference for comparing response balance. It is not an observed response value and does not necessarily represent a neutral response.
Linked definition

Net-agreement sort

3 fields changed

Linked term key: netSort

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Net-agreement sort
Definition
Not present
Net-agreement sort orders the Likert items by net agreement, the agree-side share minus the disagree-side share. It ranks items by overall favorability rather than by any single response column.
How to read it
Not present
With Most agree, read the top item as the most net-positive and the bottom as the most net-negative; Least agree flips that order. Dragging a row afterward switches the sort to Custom.
Linked definition

Bar-end summary (favorable %)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: topBox

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Bar-end summary (favorable %)
Definition
Not present
In Plot Studio, the favorable bar-end summary adds every response level on the agree side and excludes a neutral middle level; the unfavorable summary analogously adds the disagree side. Industry usage varies: 'top-box' sometimes means only the single highest category, so Plot Studio's value is more precisely an agree-side or favorable percentage.
How to read it
Not present
Read it as the share selecting any favorable level under the chart's ordered scale and center boundary. State the included response levels when reporting it.
Linked definition

Alpha (significance cutoff)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: alphaLevel

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Alpha (significance cutoff)
Definition
Not present
Alpha is the p cutoff for calling a result significant, commonly .05. Set it before testing; a smaller alpha is a stricter standard for declaring an effect.
How to read it
Not present
Read 'significant' as 'p came in below alpha,' so at alpha = .05 a p of .03 counts and a p of .07 does not.
Linked definition

Test (which one to pick)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: testChoice

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Test (which one to pick)
Definition
Not present
The menu offers mean-based and rank-based tests suited to the current comparison. Auto uses Welch's t test for independent groups and a paired t test for two measurements from the same people. Student's t test adds an equal-population-variance assumption. Mann-Whitney and Wilcoxon compare ranks and are not assumption-free versions of the t tests.
How to read it
Not present
Choose the test from the study design and the question you want to answer, not by trying several tests and keeping the smallest p value. In mixed repeated-measures analyses, Auto is useful because some rows compare the same people and others compare independent groups.
Linked definition

Multiple-comparison correction

3 fields changed

Linked term key: mcCorrections

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Multiple-comparison correction
Definition
Not present
When several related hypotheses are tested, the chance of at least one false positive can increase. Bonferroni and Holm control the chance of one or more false positives across the chosen family of tests. Benjamini-Hochberg controls the expected proportion of false positives among the results called significant, under its conditions. Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett use methods designed for particular sets of group comparisons.
How to read it
Not present
Use the corrected p value for the claim, and report both the correction method and the set of comparisons corrected together. Bonferroni, Holm, and Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted p values are never smaller than their corresponding raw p values. Tukey, Games-Howell, and Dunnett use different reference distributions, so their p values need not be larger than a p value from a separately run test.
Linked definition

Within-subject error method (Cousineau-Morey)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: cmCorrection

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Within-subject error method (Cousineau-Morey)
Definition
Not present
For repeated-measures error bars, Plot Studio first removes each person's overall level. Within each group and panel, it subtracts that person's mean across the displayed conditions and adds the grand mean across those conditions. It calculates the requested SD, SE, or CI from these normalized values and applies Morey's square-root(k/(k - 1)) correction, where k is the number of conditions. The plotted center remains the raw mean.
How to read it
Not present
This focuses the bars on within-person variation by removing stable differences between people. The bars describe one condition at a time: they are not the SE or CI of a difference between two conditions, do not measure the reliability of change, and should not be used for between-group comparisons.
Linked definition

Count (the histogram statistic)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: histStatCount

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Count (the histogram statistic)
Definition
Not present
As a Statistic choice, Count makes each bar show a raw tally: how many cases fall in that interval. Taller means more cases, and a group with more data gets taller bars overall, so counts are hard to compare across groups of different sizes.
How to read it
Not present
Read the y-axis as a number of cases; a group's bar heights add up to its sample size.
Linked definition

Proportion (the histogram statistic)

3 fields changed

Linked term key: histStatProportion

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Proportion (the histogram statistic)
Definition
Not present
As a Statistic choice, Proportion makes each bar show the share of its group's cases that fall in that interval: the bin's count divided by the group's total. The bars in a group add up to 1, so groups of different sizes can be compared fairly.
How to read it
Not present
Read a bar as the fraction of the data in that interval; multiply by 100 to talk in percents.
Linked definition

Statistic (Count / Density / Proportion)

2 fields changed

Linked term key: histStatistic

Before
After
Displayed name
Not present
Statistic (Count / Density / Proportion)
Definition
Not present
The Statistic choice sets what each histogram bar's height means: a raw count of cases, a probability density (total area 1), or the proportion of the group's cases in that interval.

No matching definition changes.

Generated 7/11/2026, 10:27:06 PM. Baseline: HEAD:inst/widget/graphbuilder2.js. Current: working-tree widget used for the installed Plot Studio 2.9.0 build.
